| SQ-186 | **Formula-valued Params rows need a runtime representation.** In particular, `prop.bond`'s TREASURY Ask slope cannot be represented by the scalar Params registry without duplicating a kernel formula outside the named key. | 13 ¬ß1; 06 ¬ß4; 08 ¬ß7 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R1 dual review) | open ‚Äî constitution Params schema owner |
| SQ-187 | **Shared Baseline semantics across late rerun windows are incomplete.** Pin which frozen window a rerun uses when its original epoch's shared Baseline has later proposal windows or an already-sealed boundary. | 04 ¬ß8.4; 05 ¬ß5.3; 06 ¬ß5.3 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R1 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 tail; verified against `pallet-market`, then documented).** Shipped behaviour is fully determined ‚Äî the spec simply never described the mechanism. The shared Baseline book stores **one window per distinct boundary triple** (`DecisionWindows: MarketId ‚Üí BoundedVec<TwapWindow, 8>`) with **per-proposal ownership layered on top** (`DecisionWindowOwners: MarketId ‚Üí BoundedVec<(ProposalId, start, trailing_start, end)>`), so sharing is by *identity of boundaries*, not by epoch: coinciding epoch-e proposals share one window and hold separate owner rows. Answering the row directly: a rerun **registers its own new window** from the fresh `decide_at` (`force_rerun_markets` recomputes `start`/`trailing_start` and calls `register_decision_window(baseline, pid, ‚Ä¶)`), never reusing or mutating its pre-rerun window; an **already-sealed** earlier window is *not* an obstacle ‚Äî it stays a distinct entry, and `consume_decision_windows` consumes **every** window a proposal owns at once ("including an earlier sealed extension attempt"), requiring all of them to be sealed first; and a rerun **cannot** read another proposal's boundaries, because every match is on the exact triple the reader owns. Eviction is reference-counted ‚Äî a window and its checkpoints drop only once the *last* Baseline-sharing owner has consumed them. The capacity edge is handled, not open: the 8-distinct-boundary ceiling (13 ¬ß4 `TwapCheckpoints`) is checked **before any mutation** and fails closed with a regression test (`ninth_twap_checkpoint_boundary_is_rejected_without_mutating_full_ring`), and `shift_decision_window` shifts a dead-man deadline **in place** precisely to avoid a transient ninth boundary on a shared Baseline. Spec trued: 04 ¬ß8.4 gained a normative five-point paragraph pinning all of it. No code change. |
| SQ-188 | **The `delta + 1pp` force-rerun rule lacks a unique producer/binding.** Confirm whether guardian force-rerun sets the same durable rerun flag as T13 and which decision window consumes the increment. | 05 ¬ß5.4; 06 ¬ß5.3 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R1 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 tail; verified against `epoch-core`, then documented).** Both halves answered, and the answer is that the code was already correct and unique ‚Äî only the spec was silent. **Producer:** both rerun kinds set the *same* durable `Proposal.rerun` flag. T13 rerun-open sets it (`crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:1171`) and the guardian `force_rerun` sets it too (`:1141`) ‚Äî notable because force-rerun goes `Trading`/`Extended`/`Queued ‚Üí Extended` **directly**, never passing through the `Rerun` state, so it would have been easy for it to be a second unflagged regime. It is not. **Uniqueness** is enforced on the producer side: `force_rerun` is admissible only while `!p.rerun && !p.delayed_once` (`:1136-1137`), which is the code form of 06 ¬ß5.3's "at most one guardian rerun of either kind per proposal, ever". **Consumer:** `decide_engine` reads the flag once (`:1786`) and applies `+ ONE_PP` to the class Œ¥ (`:1861`), so there is exactly one consuming site. **Which window:** the rerun's own Extended window and only that one ‚Äî each producer sets `decide_at := now + DECISION_EXTENSION` and `extended := true` in the same transition, so one `decide()` runs under the raised hurdle and step 6 (`!p.extended`) forbids a further extension; the hurdle can neither leak into a later window nor be inherited by a non-rerun proposal. Spec trued: 05 ¬ß5.4 gained a normative paragraph pinning the one-flag/one-window binding, and 06 ¬ß5.3's "covers both rerun kinds" sentence now says explicitly that this includes the Œ¥+1 pp regime. No code change. |

| SQ-191 | **T4 has no slash disposition for residual or implementation-unverifiable static failures.** R2 applies G-1 cancellation plus full refund and reserves 100%/10% for the cases 05 names. The PR #93 fix treats an empty or otherwise class-less payload with an empty resource declaration the same way: it is a verifiable no-op/domain mismatch, but 06 ¬ß4 names confiscation only for constitution violations and false resource declarations, so screening cancels and refunds; a class-less payload with a non-empty declaration remains a verified false declaration and is slashed 100%. Confirm the reason/disposition taxonomy. | 05 ¬ß1/¬ß2.1 T4; 06 ¬ß4; G-1; 08 ¬ß7 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R2 dual review; PR #93 bot fix) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß2.1 gained a normative *T4 disposition taxonomy* note built on one principle: **confiscation requires a verified culpable act**, so every residual, ambiguous or implementation-unverifiable static failure cancels with a full refund (G-1). 100 % slash is reserved for verified constitution violations and verified false resource declarations ‚Äî including the SQ-191 case of a class-less payload carrying a **non-empty** declaration, and (code-verified) an over-large declaration and a declared-vs-derived footprint mismatch; 10 % to INSURANCE stays scoped to preimage missing/unpinned/oversized; everything else refunds, including the class-less payload with an **empty** declaration, an undecodable payload, an unrederivable call with an admissible footprint, a missing class bond floor, a bond below a floor raised after submission, and a TREASURY ask/in-cap-prize mismatch. |
| SQ-192 | **Guard-record reaping has no assigned producer for never-queued ratified proposals or retained non-requeued reruns.** R2 makes epoch's idempotent terminal callback the universal owner; confirm that ownership in the lifecycle text. | 06 ¬ß2.2 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R2 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 06 ¬ß2.2 gained a normative *guard-record reaping ownership* paragraph confirming the shipped design: the epoch pallet's terminal callback is the **single owner**, deliberately universal rather than queue-scoped so it also covers the two stranded cases ‚Äî a proposal **ratified but never queued** (whose `RatificationRecord` would outlive it) and a **retained, non-requeued rerun**. The paragraph makes **idempotency normative** rather than incidental, which code-verification showed is load-bearing: the guard also self-cleans on its own terminal paths and several epoch call sites invoke the callback directly, so double invocation is a routine occurrence, not an edge case. |
| SQ-193 | **Guardian accountability events are outside 02 ¬ß6.** Should `ReviewFailed`/`RecallScheduled`/`ActionRatified` be appended with a 02 ¬ß13 version bump and joint sign-off, or remain off-contract chain events? | 02 ¬ß6/¬ß13 | 2026-07-17 (B1b R2 dual review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî **the underlying question is answered normatively.** 02 ¬ß6 now states its own scope: it freezes the names and field shapes of the events the canonical frontend ingests, is exhaustive for that ingest set, and is *not* an exhaustive declaration of every event a pallet may emit. An event MUST be listed iff (a) the frontend ingests it for an 11 workflow, (b) another architecture doc makes it normative, or (c) it is the sole record of a terminal proposal/vault/cohort transition; anything else MAY ship as a pallet-local diagnostic outside the contract, and ¬ß6 now names the shipped diagnostics explicitly so the boundary is never ambiguous. Applying the rule, all eight shipped guardian accountability variants are contracted (`MembersSet`, `ActionProposed`, `ActionApproved`, `ActionRatified`, `ReviewFailed`, `RecallScheduled`, `RecallEnacted`, `PlaybookRegistrationSet`). **The row's 2026-07-17 extension was over-broad and is corrected:** the three playbook lifecycle events were *already* contracted, there is no guardian `IntakePaused` variant (pause-intake rides the contracted `GuardianAction.power`), and upholding a veto emits `ActionRatified`, not a distinct uphold event. |

| SQ-160 | **Multi-limit 13 ¬ß2 rows: which enforcement unit is normative?** 15 ¬ß4.6 says "for **every** rate limit, meter, and bound, one integration test‚Ä¶" while I-22 (15 ¬ß1) says "a **registry key** without a matching test fails CI" ‚Äî for a ¬ß2 row declaring several independent numeric limits (`MinTrade`/`MaxTrade`; `prop.max_calls`/`max_bytes`/`max_weight`; PB-LEDGER-FREEZE's duration + one-renewal caps; the registry/attestor floors row; the crank-batch row) the two sentences imply different granularity (per-bound vs per-row). Confirm per-bound as the normative unit or amend one of the two sentences. | 15 ¬ß4.6; 15 ¬ß1 (I-22); 13 ¬ß2 | 2026-07-17 (S3 focused re-pass) | resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B6): per-bound ratified as the enforcement unit in 15 ¬ß4.6 and the I-22 row; the PB-LEDGER-FREEZE I-4 drift-flag clause named normatively as a gate, not a bound |
| SQ-155 | **`unwired` exemption class vs I-22's unconditional CI sentence.** 15 ¬ß4.6 / the I-22 row admit no exemption ("a registry key without a matching test fails CI" / "every meter/bound key MUST have a test"), but 7 of the 175 13-registry keys have **no consuming dispatch surface in the deployed runtime yet** (`pol.budget_epoch` meter; the three treasury obligation sync bounds; `TwapCheckpoints` ring ‚Äî a pre-existing A3/A5 gap S3 surfaced; both phase-3 caps ‚Äî production `AssetTransactor = ()`, library-level bleavit-xcm coverage exists). S3 ships them as a machine-checked `unwired` class: reason + owner **B10** mandatory, owner validated against PLAN.md milestone IDs, the full list printed on every checker run (green included), `unwired` markers never satisfy coverage, and ‚Äî added for the PR #88 Codex P1 ‚Äî **the exemption expires mechanically: the checker fails the moment an unwired key's owner milestone (or B10 for `consumer_binding`) flips ‚úÖ in PLAN.md**, so an exemption can never outlive the work that justifies it. The dead-man switch was reclassified out of this set at the same time: its enforcement CONSEQUENCE (queue freeze via the real SafetyFilter, epoch clock pause) is dispatch-tested; only the 4,800-relay-block detector that raises the flag remains B10. | SQ-200 | **05 ¬ß3.3 retention arithmetic vs the 20-snapshot window:** "reap is a precondition for pruning snapshot e‚àí20" read with cohort e reaping at e+3 (snapshots through e+2 live) implies ‚â• 22 retained epochs ‚Äî unsatisfiable at `MAX_SNAPSHOTS` = 20 without a free slot. The B4-residual seam implements cutoff `current ‚àí 19` (removes exactly ‚â§ e‚àí20, retains 19, one slot free ‚Äî regression-tested; the initially-shipped `current ‚àí 20` cutoff deadlocked settlement at ~epoch 21 and was the focused re-review's blocker). Does "e" mean the cohort epoch or the settling epoch? | 05 ¬ß3.3; 15 I-16/I-21 | 2026-07-17 (B4-residual focused re-review, blocker-driven) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß3.3's retention sentence was replaced with normative arithmetic. The doc now states outright why the alternative is unsafe ‚Äî a `current ‚àí 20` cutoff retains a full window and permanently jams `record_snapshot` (which requires strict spare capacity), deadlocking settlement into the dead-man. The "does `e` mean the cohort or settling epoch" ambiguity is removed by anchoring on the current index. |

| SQ-144 | **PB-MIGRATION checkpoint state-root semantics.** 09 ¬ß1.2 requires the parent block/state-root audit anchor, but the runtime host exposes the current transactional storage root while `frame_system` exposes the parent block hash; the exact pre-dispatch parent-state root is not named as a runtime-readable source. Is the checkpoint `(System::parent_hash, current pre-dispatch storage_root)` as implemented, or must the executive persist the parent header state root separately? | 09 ¬ß1.2; PB-MIGRATION | 2026-07-16 (B1a A8/A11 wiring) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**RULED 2026-07-20**, batch B2, jointly with SQ-127; implementation owed). Both premises confirmed and sub-claim (ii) **understated**: `sp_io::storage::root` inside `validate_block` costs a full `full_storage_root` over the *entire block's* changed-key set, is not amortized against the mandatory end-of-block root, and is **structurally** unmeasurable rather than merely unmeasured ‚Äî the benchmarking key-tracker records only `add_read_key`, which `storage_root` never calls. A third finding rode along: the `execute` benchmark fixture stopped exercising the CODE branch when B1b (`73d9d2c`) switched `prime_execute` to a PARAM payload, so the shipped weights measure a path that no longer includes the checkpoint at all, and regenerating today would silently drop its accounting. **Ruled** with SQ-127: the state-root component is deleted outright (the parent header already commits to it), the anchor is `(block_number, block_hash)` captured at application time, and 09 ¬ß3.2(2)'s "exactly the migration's writes" claim ‚Äî false for any authorize-time capture given ‚â•72 h of `DescriptorLeadTime` ‚Äî becomes true. Original refusal: The implemented pair is `(System::parent_hash(), sp_io::storage::root(...))` (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/classifier.rs:969-978`) taken mid-`do_execute` inside `with_storage_layer`, i.e. after every preceding extrinsic in the block *and* after the guard's own writes ‚Äî so the two components describe **two different states**: `parent_hash` identifies block N‚àí1 while the root identifies an intra-block N overlay. That is not a coherent audit anchor under any reading of 09 ¬ß3.2(2), and ratifying would freeze the incoherence into the spec. Two further findings: 09 ¬ß3.2(2)'s claim that the divergence set "is *exactly* the migration's writes" is **false at any authorize-time capture**, because `DescriptorLeadTime` puts ‚â• 72 h plus arbitrary keeper delay between `execute()` and application; and `sp_io::storage::root` forces a full storage-root computation inside `validate_block` whose PoV cost is unmeasured (the benchmark accounts only a 64-byte write). Resolve jointly with SQ-127: move capture to **application** time, then either take the root at block start or drop the root component and anchor on `(block_number, block_hash)`. Row citation corrected: the checkpoint lives in 09 ¬ß1.1/¬ß3.2(2), not ¬ß1.2. **Implementation owed (jointly with SQ-127):** see SQ-127 ‚Äî one change, tracked from both rows. |
| SQ-143 | **Guardian review/recall scheduling and effect dispatch cannot be atomic through the frozen seams.** `GuardianReviewScheduler`/`GuardianRecallScheduler` return infallible `u32` after the guardian action has already been persisted, so a failed stock-referenda submission cannot roll the action back; guardian storage also reaps the action‚Üíproposal binding needed by `review_window_closed(pid)`, and the pallet records an approved action without a sibling-effect dispatch seam. Should these traits become fallible/transactional and retain the pid binding, or should a separate bounded machinery crank own submission/effects? | 06 ¬ß5‚Äì¬ß6; 15 I-23 | 2026-07-16 (B1a A8/A11 wiring) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (triage sweep ‚Äî code-verified).** B1b made both seams fallible and transactional: the traits return `Result<u32, DispatchError>` with the contract stated inline ‚Äî "scheduling failure must roll back the maintenance step" (`pallets/guardian/src/lib.rs:146-182`); the runtime performs a real `Preimage::bound` ‚Üí `Referenda::submit` ‚Üí `place_decision_deposit` and returns the actual referendum index (`configs.rs:4459-4489`, `:4511-4540`); every dispatchable that can reach it is wrapped in `with_storage_layer`, so a failed submission un-persists the guardian action. The action‚Üíproposal binding moved out of reapable guardian storage into epoch-owned `GuardianReviewDeadlines` (`configs.rs:2812-2816`), and effects dispatch through `EffectDispatcher`/`ProposalVeto`. No `u32::MAX` sentinel survives. |
| SQ-142 | **Epoch market deployment/decision telemetry has no complete production projection.** `MarketAccess::open_markets` needs canonical collision-free market ids/accounts/POL parameters, while `decision_grade` needs exact decision-window TWAP coverage, staleness, contest notional/POL, measured depth/flow, second-insufficiency, and previous settled Baseline data; `pallet-market` currently exposes only books, `BaselineMarketOf`, and the last quote. Where should the bounded telemetry/checkpoint state and id allocator live? | 04 ¬ß1‚Äì¬ß5; 05 ¬ß5 | 2026-07-16 (B1a A8/A11 wiring) | open (narrowed 2026-07-20, triage sweep) ‚Äî **every listed input but one is now a real read**, and the SQ's own question is answered: the bounded telemetry/checkpoint state and the id allocator live in `pallet-market` (`NextMarketId`, `DecisionWindows`, `DecisionWindowOwners`, `TwapCheckpoints`). Ids/accounts/POL params come from `allocate_market_id` + `market_book_account` + the frozen `seed_plan`; coverage, staleness, contest notional/POL, measured depth, second-insufficiency and the previous settled Baseline all resolve through `register_decision_window` + `decision_grade_facts_at` + `RecentCohortSummaries`. **Residual**: `published_flow_per_day` returns `None` with no production writer (`configs.rs:2693-2701`), so the kernel rides the *specified* LÃÇ/2 fallback (05 ¬ß5.6 / 08 ¬ß5.2) ‚Äî deliberate; the two adjacent floor fallbacks are owned by SQ-177 and SQ-173. |
| SQ-141 | **Measurement context is many-version while registry asks for one version, and several economic inputs lack owners.** Concurrent cohorts may freeze multiple MetricSpec versions for one measurement epoch, but `registry::EpochContext::frozen_spec_version` returns one `u16`; no bounded epoch-indexed source retains historical epoch-end timing; additionally no runtime storage presently owns `StakeAtRisk(c,m)` cohort escrow, registry milestone targets, welfare normalized on-chain/relay daily counters, or the certified `InCapPrize` envelopes for PARAM/CODE/META. Should the registry key become versioned and which pallets own these bounded inputs/timing? | 05 ¬ß4‚Äì¬ß6; 07 ¬ß2/¬ß6; 08 ¬ß5.2 | 2026-07-16 (B1a A8/A11 wiring) | open ‚Äî ambiguous/missing values return 0/None or an incomplete component set, so admission/settlement fails closed; settled oracle components and incident multipliers are wired. |
| SQ-140 | **Qualification-time preimage ownership has no A8 lifecycle seam.** 09 ¬ß7.3 explicitly requires epoch to request the preimage at qualification and release it at every terminal state, but A8 exposes only a length read and A11 cleanup no-ops before a Queue entry exists. The runtime can therefore pin only atomically at successful guard enqueue (and safely unrequest at guard terminal), leaving the normative qualification‚Üíqueue pin interval and prequeue-terminal release unimplemented without changing the pallet seam. Direct unrequest of an unrequested hash also debug-panics in `pallet-preimage`. Should A8 gain explicit pin/unpin hooks and terminal coverage? | 09 ¬ß7.3, ¬ß1.1/¬ß1.5; 15 I-9 | 2026-07-16 (B1a A8/A11 wiring) | **resolved 2026-07-17 (B5 review fix):** A8's preimage seam now has idempotent request/unrequest hooks; qualification requests atomically, every pre-queue terminal releases, successful enqueue hands ownership to A11 atomically, and try-state/tests cover orphan/leak prevention. |
| SQ-205 | **Reserve health never reaches the treasury: 08 ¬ß1.2's fail-static NAV is not enforced (high).** `pallet-oracle::reserve_unhealthy()` is the authoritative `R` flag (07 ¬ß8), and `pallet-futarchy-treasury::set_reserve_impaired(flag)` exists with the comment "wired at B1a" ‚Äî but **no production caller connects them** (only core tests call it). So while the oracle reports unhealthy reserves, `Treasury.reserve_impaired` stays false, `nav()` reports `haircut_flag: false` with an unhaircut `spendable_nav`, and every 08 ¬ß4 minimum-viable-NAV gate keeps admitting new commitments ‚Äî the opposite of 08 ¬ß1.2(2)'s "spendable NAV for all new commitments is 0 ‚Ä¶ fail-static". This is a chain-behavior gap, not a view defect: B2's `nav()` faithfully reports the flag the admission checks themselves use (B2 deliberately did NOT override it in the view, which would have made the API disagree with what the chain enforces). Note the observable symptom: `welfare_current().reserve_flag` (authoritative oracle) and `nav().haircut_flag` (unwired mirror) can disagree, though 02 ¬ß4 says the latter follows the former. Wire the seam transactionally (oracle probe result ‚Üí treasury), or single-home the flag so both read one source. Fails a release closed via the manifest `release_blockers` row `treasury.reserve_health_unwired`. | 08 ¬ß1.2; 07 ¬ß8; 02 ¬ß4 (`NavView.haircut_flag`, `WelfareView.reserve_flag`) | 2026-07-17 (B2 adversarial review) | open ‚Äî owner B1a (the seam its own comments claim it wired); release-blocking |
| SQ-206 | **`ParamView.max_delta: u128` cannot represent a non-absolute max-Œî rule.** 13 ¬ß1's max-Œî column carries three kinds (absolute, percent, factor); 02 ¬ß4 projects them onto one `u128` scalar with no stated semantics. `MaxDelta::Factor` is asymmetric (`[ceil(v/f), v¬∑f]`), so *any* single scalar is lossy: B2 ships the conservative `min(upward, downward)` (R-7 ‚Äî never render an inadmissible amendment as admissible), which under-reports the upward allowance, e.g. `exec.lock.code` (100,800, √ó2) reports 50,400 though 201,600 is admissible. Should `ParamView` carry exact `min_next`/`max_next` (or the tagged rule) instead of one scalar? That is a 02 ¬ß4 contract amendment. | 02 ¬ß4 (`ParamView`); 13 ¬ß1 + reading rule 2; 15 I-6/INV-FE-2 | 2026-07-17 (B2 dual review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `ParamView` gains trailing `min_next` and `max_next`: the exact inclusive interval of admissible next values, obtained by intersecting the record's `min`/`max` with its max-Œî rule, with factor rounding exactly as the admission code does (`ceil(value/factor)` down, saturating `value √ó factor` up) and no-rule yielding the full `[min, max]`. `max_delta` is retained and normatively defined as the conservative symmetric projection `min(upward, downward)`. The row's example verified exactly: `exec.lock.code` = 100,800 √ó2 projects to 50,400 while 201,600 is admissible ‚Äî under-reporting the upward allowance, never over-reporting it (R-7-safe, but a real functional gap for 15 I-6 and 11's constitution dashboard). |
| SQ-207 | **Is the INSURANCE account inside NAV?** 08 ¬ß1.1 lists INSURANCE among the treasury's lines ("inflow-only except by TREASURY decision"), but `BudgetLine` has no `Insurance` variant, no treasury call spends it, and 13 ¬ß4's line enumeration omits it. Consequently `NavView.total` (treasury-core's own arithmetic) **excludes** the insurance custody while `NavView.insurance` reports it ‚Äî the 02 ¬ß4 field set reads as an account decomposition of `total` (11 ¬ß11.8.3 renders it as such) but does not sum to it. Excluding it understates NAV, which is the conservative/fail-static direction for spending gates, so B2 ships that and does not touch A9's solvency math. If insurance is in NAV, A9 needs a `BudgetLine::Insurance` + custody sync; if not, 08 ¬ß1.2 should say so and 11 ¬ß11.8.3's decomposition should exclude it. | 08 ¬ß1.1/¬ß1.2; 02 ¬ß4 (`NavView`); 13 ¬ß4; 11 ¬ß11.8.3 | 2026-07-17 (B2 dual review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**reclassified 2026-07-20**, B4 ratification ‚Äî refused; implementation owed). The accounting half is settled and ratified ‚Äî 08 ¬ß1.2 now states normatively that INSURANCE is **outside** NAV, that excluding it is the fail-static direction for every cap and floor that divides by NAV, and that `NavView.insurance` is transparency-only and not a summand of `total`. But the row cannot close, because the reason it reads as an accounting question is a defect: the INSURANCE balance is **unreachable by any dispatchable, permanently**. No `BudgetLine::Insurance` variant exists, `spend`/`open_stream`/`fund_budget_line` all require one, and `recover_foreign` explicitly refuses USDC and VIT (`crates/futarchy-treasury-core/src/lib.rs:845-848`). So 08 ¬ß1.1's ‚Äúinflow-only *except by TREASURY decision*‚Äù has no implementation, and slashed bonds, vault residue and reclaimed position deposits accumulate forever in a dead account ‚Äî functionally the burn that 08 ¬ß7.1 rejects by name as stranding backing reserve. Fix is either `BudgetLine::Insurance` + custody sync (no `NavView` change, so no contract bump) or an explicit doc ruling that INSURANCE is a permanent sink, which is a values call. |
| SQ-208 | **`PositionView.vault_state` has no branch-free settled representation.** 02 ¬ß2's `VaultState::ScalarSettled { winner, s }` requires a `Branch`, but a Baseline vault (03 ¬ß2.3) settles with a scalar and no branch ‚Äî so B2's `account_positions` must fabricate `winner: Branch::Accept` for Baseline positions and rely on consumers ignoring a field 02 never says is meaningless. 02 ¬ß4 nonetheless binds the field ("Baseline: its epoch vault"). Pre-genesis, so appending a `BaselineSettled { s }` variant to `VaultState` is available; alternatives are making the field optional or stating normatively that `winner` is undefined for `PositionId::Baseline`. | 02 ¬ß2 (`VaultState`), 02 ¬ß4 (`PositionView`); 03 ¬ß2.3 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `VaultState` gains a trailing branch-free **`BaselineSettled { s: FixedU64 }`** variant; proposal vaults keep `ScalarSettled { winner, s }`, and `PositionView` uses the new variant for `PositionId::Baseline`. This replaces the shipped projection that fabricated `winner: Branch::Accept` for every Baseline position ‚Äî publishing a winning branch that does not exist is exactly what R-7 forbids, and "declare `winner` undefined" would still have forced every generated client to carry and special-case false data. Ledger storage was already branch-free (`BaselineState::{Open, Settled(s)}`); only the contract and its projection were lying. |
| SQ-209 | **`RatificationStatus::Pending { referendum }` is unreachable, and "no referendum yet" has no spelling.** 06 ¬ß2.2(2) has the chain learn of a ratification referendum only when it *passes* (the scheduler dispatches `execution_guard.ratify`), so no on-chain record exists for an in-flight one. B2 therefore renders a ratification-requiring proposal with no record as the guard's fail-closed `Failed { referendum: 0 }` from submission onward ‚Äî including Intake/Trading, where 06 ¬ß2.2(1) says the referendum legitimately may not exist yet. Conservative (and it replaced a genuine `NotRequired` defect), but neither variant means "absent". Should 02 ¬ß2 gain an `Absent`/`NotSubmitted` variant (pre-genesis), or is `Failed { referendum: 0 }` the intended encoding? | 02 ¬ß2 (`RatificationStatus`); 06 ¬ß2.2 | 2026-07-17 (B2 dual review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `RatificationStatus` is restructured pre-genesis to **`NotRequired`, `NoPassedRecord`, `Passed { referendum }`**. `Pending { referendum }` was **unreachable by construction** ‚Äî 06 ¬ß2.2(2) has the chain learn of a ratification referendum only when it passes, so no on-chain record of an in-flight one exists ‚Äî and freezing dead surface is worse than removing it before genesis. `NoPassedRecord` is deliberately agnostic between "none submitted", "ongoing" and "failed", because the guard's only write is the passing record and it **cannot prove** the stronger fact; that is why it is not named `NotSubmitted` or `Absent`. It replaces the shipped `Failed { referendum: 0 }` encoding, which rendered a normal Intake/Trading proposal as though its ratification had failed. The frontend derives the referendum lifecycle from `pallet-referenda`, as 11 already requires. |
| SQ-210 | **`quote()`'s failure representation is unspecified.** 02 ¬ß3/¬ß4 give `quote()` a total return (`QuoteView`, not `Option`), so a missing/closed/overflowing/out-of-domain book needs a sentinel. B2 returns `cost: 0, fee: 0, p_after_1e9: 0` with `within_domain: false` ‚Äî a **zero charge for a buy**, i.e. the anti-maker-adverse direction if a client ever renders it as a price, with `within_domain` overloaded to mean both "domain exceeded" and "not evaluable". Is the zero sentinel intended, should it be `Balance::MAX`, or should `QuoteView` gain an explicit evaluability field? | 02 ¬ß3/¬ß4 (`quote`/`QuoteView`); 04 ¬ß4 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `QuoteView` gains a trailing **`evaluable: bool`**. `evaluable = true` for any successfully computed quote *regardless of* `within_domain`; `false` for a missing, closed, trade-inadmissible, inventory-invalid or overflowing book, in which case every price-like field is non-renderable and non-actionable. `within_domain` keeps its single meaning: the post-trade domain predicate. **Two corrections:** the row's claim that an out-of-domain book gets the sentinel is false ‚Äî `market-core` computes a real quote and sets `within_domain` independently, so only a computation error yields zeros; and `Balance::MAX` was **rejected** as the sentinel because the same view covers sells, where that value is adverse in the opposite direction. |
| SQ-211 | **"Active MetricSpec version" is never defined, and `0` is overloaded.** 02 ¬ß3 promises `welfare_current()` reports the "active MetricSpec"; 05 ¬ß4.6 gives only `activation_epoch ‚â• current + 2` and I-16. B2 now routes the view through the same canonical selector the qualification path uses (greatest `activation_epoch`, unique, else fail-closed `None`), but the view's total return forces a sentinel `spec_version: 0` for "no active spec" ‚Äî and `0` is a legal `MetricSpecVersion`. Which selector is normative, and how should "none active" be encoded? | 02 ¬ß3/¬ß4 (`WelfareView`); 05 ¬ß4.6; 15 I-16 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `WelfareView` gains a trailing **`active_spec_available: bool`**; `spec_version` is meaningful only when it is true, and it is true even when the selected version is `0`, which stays a legal `MetricSpecVersion`. This retires the overloaded `spec_version: 0` sentinel rather than outlawing a legal identifier to preserve it. 05 ¬ß4.6 now states the shipped selector normatively ‚Äî among versions whose complete component set is active at the queried epoch, the unique version with the latest activation epoch; no candidate, or a tie, means no active spec ‚Äî which is already the one selector shared by the qualification path and the view. |
| SQ-212 | **`ParamView.value`'s unit for Perbill/Percent/Fixed keys.** 02 ¬ß4 says "SCALE-encoded per-key scalar, unit per 13"; 13 ¬ß1's `mkt.fee` row reads `Type: Perbill \| Unit: bps \| Default: 30`, and genesis stores `Perbill(3_000_000)`. So `params()[mkt.fee].value` is 3,000,000 while the `Market::Fee` metadata constant is 30 ‚Äî and 02 ¬ß9 binds the FE to **both** for the same purpose ("quote display, buy/sell cost recompute"). A client trusting 13's "bps" unit computes a 300√ó fee (it fails closed via 11 ¬ß11.5's cross-check ‚Üí `FE-CHAIN-005`, but trading blocks). Which unit does `ParamView.value` carry? | 02 ¬ß4/¬ß9; 13 ¬ß1 (`mkt.fee`) | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî 02 ¬ß4 now defines `ParamView.value`/`.min`/`.max` as the **raw inner scalar of the stored `ParamValue`** (`Fixed` on the 1e9 grid, `Perbill` in parts per billion, `Percent` as integer percent, integer/balance variants native) ‚Äî explicitly *not* the human unit printed in 13's Unit column, which 13 now says so too. The canonical `Perbill`‚Üíbps conversion (**divide by 100,000**, floored as shipped) is stated, and 02 ¬ß9 / 11 ¬ß11.5 P-1 bind fee recomputation to the frozen `Market::Fee` bps projection cross-checked against the raw parameter. **The row's "300√ó" is false** ‚Äî raw 3,000,000 misread as bps instead of 30 is **100,000√ó**. No production code needed; the raw-view/bps-constant split already ships. |
| SQ-213 | **Is `decision_stats()` meant to be servable before the decision boundary?** 02 ¬ß3 says "exactly as `decide()` would read them **now**", and 02 ¬ß9 lists the `dec.*` keys as backing "decision previews". B2's view returns `None` for the whole Trade phase (the full-window TWAP has no checkpoint at a future boundary) and at the boundary itself while registered windows are still unsealed (the crank syncs the clock and seals first, then decides ‚Äî the view reads immediately). Conservative, but it makes the preview unavailable exactly where 02 ¬ß9 implies it is wanted. Also: `backing_complete` requires live baseline TWAPs even though `decide` may legally use the 05 ¬ß5.3 previous-settled-baseline carry, so a decidable proposal can read `None`. Should a preview read a different window, or is `None`-until-sealed the contract? | 02 ¬ß3 (`decision_stats`), 02 ¬ß9; 05 ¬ß5.2-¬ß5.4 | 2026-07-17 (B2 dual review) | open ‚Äî **ruled 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6); the code half reclassified to X.** Ratified in the contract: `decision_stats()` returns `None` until the registered decision windows are sealed and every input the decision path needs is evaluable ‚Äî it is a **finalized** decision-statistics view, not an in-Trade projected TWAP, and 02 ¬ß9's "decision previews" wording plus 11's market/proposal screens are re-trued to stop implying otherwise. Inventing a different preview window would have been inventing semantics (R-2). **The second half is a real code defect and stays open:** `backing_complete` unconditionally requires live Baseline full *and* trailing values even though `decide` legally uses the previous-settled carry, so a decidable proposal can read `None`. Owed in X: Baseline backing is complete when either the live decision-grade reads exist or the carry exists and is the value `decide` will use, keeping every other fail-closed check. |
| SQ-214 | **`OracleRoundView.escalated: bool` is undefined.** 02 ¬ß4 names the field without semantics. B2 reads it as "a prior round escalated the game" (`round > 1`, coherent with oracle-core incrementing `round` and clearing `challenger` on escalation); the alternative ‚Äî "a challenge is live" (`challenger.is_some()`) ‚Äî differs during a round-1 challenge that 07 ¬ß5.2 says will escalate at window close. Which does the FE oracle-ballot screen (11 ¬ß11.8) need? | 02 ¬ß4 (`OracleRoundView`); 07 ¬ß5.2 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî 02 ¬ß4 defines `OracleRoundView.escalated` as **`round > 1`**: true iff at least one prior round advanced the game, and **false for a round-1 report even while that round has a live challenger**. The contract states explicitly that it does *not* mean "currently challenged" ‚Äî a live challenge is a different fact, and a future view needing it must add a separately named field. Ratified as shipped: `views.rs` already emits exactly this meaning and a runtime test pins both arms. **The row's framing is corrected** ‚Äî neither reading of one bool can satisfy 11 ¬ß11.7.5's ballot screen, which needs full dispute lineage from round storage and events. |
| SQ-215 | **02 ¬ß7.5's attestor bounds have no 13 ¬ß4 row and no reap path (liveness trap).** The v4 freeze pins `Members ‚â§ 16` / `Attestations ‚â§ 256` ‚Äî bounds rule 3 says must come from 13 ¬ß4, which has no attestor row ‚Äî and the flat 256-entry ledger is never reaped (06 ¬ß2.2 reaps ratification records with the proposal; there is no attestation analogue). Once full, `attest` is a permanent no-op ‚áí no CODE/META proposal can reach quorum (05 ¬ß5.4 step 10) ‚áí the CODE upgrade that would fix it cannot itself qualify. Add a 13 ¬ß4 attestor bounds row and a reap-with-proposal path (a shape change 02 ¬ß7.5 must then version), or make the freeze conditional on one. | 02 ¬ß7.5; 13 ¬ß4; rule 3; 06 ¬ß2.2 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | open ‚Äî B2 froze the shipped shape; the reaper is unowned |
| SQ-216 | **02 ¬ß9's Status column is implementation status inside the versioned ¬ß2-¬ß12 range.** 02 ¬ß13(2) bumps `INTEGRATION_CONTRACT_VERSION` on "any change to ¬ß2-¬ß12", so flipping a row from "Next wiring" to "Wired" would nominally require a version bump for a status edit ‚Äî and R-4 says status belongs in PLAN.md, not the spec. Should the column be dropped from 02 (status tracked in PLAN.md), or is it explicitly exempt from ¬ß13(2)? | 02 ¬ß9; 02 ¬ß13(2); AGENTS.md R-4 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî **the Status column and the "Wired"/"Next wiring" definitions are deleted from 02 ¬ß9.** Every row was wired, so the column carried only stale implementation prose inside a range whose every textual change is versioned by ¬ß13(2) ‚Äî the exact R-4 violation the row identifies. No ¬ß13(2) exemption was created: an exemption would have preserved the duplication and weakened the otherwise simple "any ¬ß2‚Äì¬ß12 change bumps" rule. Status lives in PLAN.md. |
| SQ-217 | **02 ¬ß9's "canonical name freeze" omits ~17 shipped metadata constants** across `pallet-welfare` (`MaxMetricSpecs`/`MaxSnapshots`/`MaxGateFlags`/`MaxDailyGateSamples`), `pallet-futarchy-treasury` (`MaxStreams`/`MaxBudgetLines`/`MaxPolCommitments`), `pallet-guardian` (`GuardianSeats`/`GuardianThreshold`/`GuardianBond`/`ReviewDeadlineEpochs`), `pallet-attestor` (`AttMinMembers`/`AttQuorum`/`AttestorBond`/`ChallengeWindowBlocks`) and the per-pallet `INTEGRATION_CONTRACT_VERSION` mirrors. The table reasons about only one omission class (PalletIds, "intentionally absent"). Either enumerate them or extend the rationale. | 02 ¬ß9 (SQ-138 name freeze) | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî 02 ¬ß9's name freeze is completed with the **16 verified omitted constants**: Welfare (`INTEGRATION_CONTRACT_VERSION`, `MaxMetricSpecs`, `MaxSnapshots`, `MaxGateFlags`, `MaxDailyGateSamples`), FutarchyTreasury (`INTEGRATION_CONTRACT_VERSION`, `MaxStreams`, `MaxBudgetLines`, `MaxPolCommitments`), Guardian (`GuardianSeats`, `GuardianThreshold`, `GuardianBond`, `PlaybookFreezeWindowBlocks`), Attestor (`AttMinMembers`, `AttQuorum`, `ChallengeWindowBlocks`). **The row's inventory was materially wrong and was not copied:** `ReviewDeadlineEpochs` is a live `Config::Get` seam, not a metadata constant; there is no `AttestorBond` constant; the shipped Guardian constant the row misses is `PlaybookFreezeWindowBlocks`; and only Welfare and FutarchyTreasury carry contract-version mirrors among the four. 16, not ~17. |
| SQ-218 | **`epoch_status()` hydrates the whole epoch aggregate for a 7-scalar view.** The read goes through `pallet_epoch::epoch_state()`, which loads all proposals, cohorts, the 32-entry ring, resource locks, rollovers and params (~130 storage reads) to produce a ‚â§128 B view 02 ¬ß3 calls O(bounded-collection) ‚Äî correct, but this is the most-called view (countdowns/phase headers) and every read enters the `chainHead_call` proof a light client downloads (D-6). Worth a narrow `status_view` reader over `EpochOf`/`Schedule` + the three flags? | 02 ¬ß3 (bounds/size); 01 ¬ß5.1; D-6 | 2026-07-17 (B2 spec-review) | open ‚Äî performance, not correctness |
| SQ-131 | **Relay `UpgradeGoAhead::Abort` recovery semantics are unspecified.** Stable2603 Cumulus deletes `PendingValidationCode` on Abort **without any runtime callback** (verified: `cumulus-pallet-parachain-system-0.26.0` src/lib.rs:739), after frame-system has already consumed `AuthorizedUpgrade` ‚Äî without handling, guard `PendingUpgrade` wedges every future upgrade (`PendingUpgradeExists`). **B6 ruling (G-1 status-quo default, needs ratification):** the runtime detects the abort deterministically (scheduling latch + installed-code-hash check in the validation-data hook), clears guard `PendingUpgrade`/checkpoint + the ReleaseChannel pending fields, emits append-only `UpgradeAborted{code_hash}`, raises a guardian-visible halt-source bit, and the upgrade is **re-proposable through the normal process** ‚Äî no automatic re-authorization (the relay said no). 09 ¬ß2 should gain the Abort arm. | 09 ¬ß2.1(4)‚Äì(6); G-1 | 2026-07-17 (B6 dual review, blocker) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** 09 ¬ß2.1 gained arm **(7)**: deterministic abort detection in the validation-data hook (scheduling latch + installed-code-hash mismatch), clearing `PendingUpgrade` and the checkpoint, tolerant clearing of the `ReleaseChannel` pending fields, append-only `UpgradeAborted { code_hash }`, a guardian-visible incident bit that does **not** by itself freeze the queue, and re-proposal through the normal process with no automatic re-authorization. False-positive analysis came back clean (five conjunctive conditions; the Abort signal is re-read from the relay proof every block; re-fire impossible once `PendingUpgrade` is `None`). Two row corrections rode the ratification: the Cumulus citation was stale (the pin is **0.29.0**, `src/lib.rs:786-789`, not 0.26.0), and a *clean* abort raises the incident bit **without** halting ‚Äî only a failed cleanup halts, which the row conflated. Adding `UpgradeAborted` to 02 ¬ß6 needs the contract bump and rides batch **C**. |
| SQ-132 | **PB-MIGRATION machine-trigger set has three unresolved edges.** (a) Trigger arm 3 "post-upgrade `try-state` assertion failure signal" has **no on-chain signal source** (`try-state` does not run in production block execution) ‚Äî what is the intended machine-checked source? (b) Does "`try-state` must pass before the freeze lifts" (09 ¬ß3.2(3)) bind the on-chain `MigrationHalt` flag or only the guardian playbook freeze? B6 clears migration-source halts on MBM completion / successful recovery-image application without a try-state run. (c) B6 raises the halt on two **non-enumerated** conditions (applied-code mismatch; relay Abort per SQ-131) ‚Äî conservative, but 09 ¬ß3.2's trigger list is closed and should be amended. (d) The normative definition of "cursor **stalled**": B6 uses `(index, inner_cursor)` byte-equality for > `MIGRATION_STALL_BLOCKS` (900); a migration lawfully mutating storage while returning identical cursor bytes would false-raise (self-clears on completion). | 09 ¬ß3.2; 15 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-17 (B6 dual review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**RULED 2026-07-20**, batch B2: (a)/(b)/(c) ratified, (d) ruled; implementation owed). 09 ¬ß3.2 gained a trigger-list amendment: **(a)** the "post-upgrade `try-state` assertion failure signal" arm is **retired** as an on-chain trigger ‚Äî `try-state` never runs in production block execution on any Substrate runtime (every `frame-executive` call site is `#[cfg(feature = "try-runtime")]`), so it was unimplementable rather than unimplemented; it survives as a CI release gate and an O5 operator signal. **(b)** ¬ß3.2(3)'s "`try-state` must pass before the freeze lifts" is bound to the **guardian playbook freeze**, not the on-chain `MigrationHalt` flag, which follows from (a). **(c)** The two previously non-enumerated halt conditions (applied-code mismatch; failed abort cleanup) are now enumerated, and the clean-abort case is documented as bit-only. Sharper than the row: production wires `type Migrations = ()`, so the *original* arms 1‚Äì2 are structurally unreachable today and every reachable halt came from a non-enumerated condition. **(d) refused:** byte-equality of `(index, inner_cursor)` was not ratified ‚Äî a lawful `Cursor = ()` migration draining a map false-raises, and the consequences are worse than "self-clears": the halt makes the **expedited CODE lane admissible**, and `retire_stuck_migration_cursor_for_remediation` (`configs.rs:499-513`) will **kill a live, lawfully-progressing cursor** when the remediation upgrade applies. **Residual: (d)** ‚Äî needs a cursor-advance obligation on registered MBMs or a progress-independent definition, plus normative text for the retirement interaction. **Implementation owed:** replace the byte-equality detector with `now ‚àí cursor.started_at > MIGRATION_STALL_BLOCKS`, deleting `MigrationProgressMarker` and a blake2_256 from the mandatory inherent; add the integrity test requiring every registered MBM to declare `max_steps()` **strictly** below `MIGRATION_STALL_BLOCKS`; emit `MigrationHalted { cursor, failed_step }`, which 09 ¬ß3.2(4) mandates and which exists nowhere in the workspace; cover `retire_stuck_migration_cursor_for_remediation` and `active_migration_stall_is_live`, which have zero tests. Latent until the first real MBM (`type Migrations = ()`), so schedule before one ships. **Residual noted 2026-07-21:** `ActiveCursor::goto_next_migration` resets `started_at` per migration, so both the SDK budget and the ruled predicate are **per-segment**. A chain of *k* individually-compliant migrations therefore holds the chain in `OnlyInherents` for up to k √ó (`MIGRATION_STALL_BLOCKS` ‚àí 1) blocks with no detector firing at all. 09 ¬ß3.2(d) defines stalled per-segment only; whether a run-level budget is also wanted rides this row (it interacts with SQ-309). |
| SQ-133 | **02 ¬ß12 `updated_at` (offset 108) writer-(a) semantics.** 02 ¬ß12 calls it "block number of last update", but the writer-(a) field enumeration (spec_version target, pending_authorized_at, URGENT_UPGRADE, clear-pending) excludes it ‚Äî a guard write would leave it stale. **B6 ruling (needs ratification):** guard writes bump `updated_at` to the current block (plain-meaning reading; harmless to stranded readers ‚Äî `manifest_txid` is untouched). If instead the field is writer-(b)-only manifest freshness, revert the two adapter sites. | 02 ¬ß12; 09 ¬ß2.3 | 2026-07-17 (B6 dual review, split ruling) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** The plain reading stands and **no 02 change was needed**, so no contract bump: 02 ¬ß12's row-108 gloss ("block number of last update") is unqualified, and the per-writer parentheticals enumerate the fields each writer sets *purposefully* rather than granting exclusive title to an offset. The clarifying sentence went into **09 ¬ß2.3**, which is where 12 ¬ß106 routes write authority and which is outside the versioned surface. Verified harmless before ratifying: offset 108 has exactly one consumer ‚Äî a bare gauge in `tools/monitoring/chain_alerts_exporter.py:416` ‚Äî no alert rule references it, the 12 ¬ß6.3 repoint-lag alert derives from `manifest_txid` divergence instead, and the field appears in no view type and no runtime API. Row correction: there are **three** guard write sites, not "the two adapter sites" ‚Äî authorization, applied detection, and the SQ-131 abort cleanup. |
| SQ-134 | **02 ¬ß12 writer field-scoping is ambiguous.** `constitution.set_release_channel` (writer (b)) accepts an arbitrary 168-byte value and can therefore overwrite the guard-owned pending fields (offsets 112‚Äì120, flag bit 2) . Is the per-writer field enumeration **normative** (‚áí `set_release_channel` must preserve guard-owned offsets) or informative? **Hardened 2026-07-17 (final verify + PR #65 bot P1):** both guard clears ‚Äî abort AND applied ‚Äî are now tolerant (a mismatched channel is left untouched as writer (b)'s newer authoritative value; guard state still clears), so a mid-flight rewrite can no longer wedge `PendingUpgrade` on either path; regressions `relay_abort_cleanup_survives_a_writer_b_release_channel_rewrite` + `applied_cleanup_survives_a_writer_b_release_channel_rewrite`. Residual: a rewrite still erases the FE-visible pending display until the ruling lands. | 02 ¬ß12 | 2026-07-17 (B6 spec-reviewer) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî **the batch's highest-severity ruling; text and code landed together.** 02 ¬ß12's self-contradiction is fixed by ruling offset 112 `spec_version` to be the **currently installed** runtime version, always, never the target ‚Äî the direction the field exists for, since a stranded metadata-less reader uses it for runtime compatibility. Field ownership is now **normative**: the guard exclusively owns offsets 112‚Äì119 and flags bit 2; `set_release_channel` MUST preserve them and now *merges* rather than replacing the whole record. The guard's three occasions are enumerated exhaustively (authorize / applied / **relay abort**, which ¬ß12 omitted), the clears are unconditional on the guard's own pending state, and the content predicate that latched them is deleted. New **I-30** binds `PendingUpgrade.is_some()` ‚ü∫ `pending_authorized_at != 0` ‚ü∫ bit 2, enforced in try-state; 09 ¬ß2.3's "neither writer exclusive title" statement is reversed. ¬ß12's risk text is corrected too: writer (b) could previously **suppress a live pending upgrade**, not merely raise a false banner. **The row's own claim was overstated and is not ratified:** the phantom was persistent-until-next-write, not permanent ‚Äî writer (b) could clear it directly and a later authorization overwrites it. All eight combinations of (`PendingUpgrade`, `pending_authorized_at`, `URGENT_UPGRADE`) were reachable; the shipped regressions covered only the zero-pending arm, so tests for both bad directions were added. |

| SQ-264 | **Production genesis seats for the coretime quote authority are ops-ceremony inputs.** B12 wires `coretime_quote_authority`/`coretime_renewal_account` as genesis-set treasury storage (dev presets use the dev ops stand-ins; unset fails closed), but the production genesis-allocation template and chain-spec validator carry no seats for the two values ‚Äî they are outputs of the Phase-2/3 ops-multisig + Coretime-account ceremony that 09 ¬ß4 already `[VERIFY]`-tags. Omission is fail-closed (no quote can be noted, renewal stays inert), so this is an onboarding input, not a safety gap. Add the two fields to `deploy/genesis/allocations.template.json` + validator when the ceremony defines them. | 09 ¬ß4 | 2026-07-18 (B12 spec-review minor) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; onboarding input made a gated obligation).** Confirmed on all four sub-claims: the two genesis fields exist, dev presets seat stand-ins, unset genuinely fails closed (no account can note a quote; renewal stays inert), and the production template and validator carry no seats at all. Ratified that neither can be a pre-fixed constant ‚Äî both are *outputs* of the Phase-2/3 ceremony ‚Äî but the row's "add them when the ceremony defines them" disposition is **strengthened**, because fail-closed here is also silent: a genesis that simply forgets them is indistinguishable from one awaiting the ceremony. 09 ¬ß4 now requires the production template and chain-spec validator to carry both as **explicit unfilled seats** (wired, with tests), and 12 ¬ß6.5 makes seating them a **Phase-3 entry gate** proven by one end-to-end quote note + prune. Nuance recorded: 09 ¬ß4's "genesis-set to the ops multisig" described an intent with no production wiring behind it, which is now an onboarding obligation rather than a claim. |

B13's telemetry ruling (this session, decide-yourself delegation): the exported `bleavit_runtime_migration_cursor_stalled` signal is the **union** of the no-progress detector and the failure-halt/`Stuck` cursor state ‚Äî a permanently failed cursor is strictly more alarming than a slow one, and RB-UPGRADE owns both; the underlying normative "stalled" semantics for PB-MIGRATION remain open. | 12 ¬ß6.3; 05 ¬ß4.3 | 2026-07-18 (B13 spec-review minor + its SPEC-QUESTION 2) | resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B6): 12 ¬ß6.3 now defines the signal normatively as the union of the stall-halt latch, the live no-progress detector and the `Stuck` cursor, and states that the no-progress leg admits false positives by design. Row corrected ‚Äî `MIGRATION_FAILURE_HALT` is not an input to the union |

B13 ruling (this session, decide-yourself delegation): the two series carry a `component` label (`pol` = Pol line vs Œ£ live-book commitments; `baseline` = PolBaseline line vs the standing next-Baseline `seed_headroom(pol.b_baseline)`) so the rule expr compares per component and masking is impossible. | SQ-247 | **Does a 05 T13 rerun's extra POL consume the current epoch's `pol.budget_epoch`?** 08 ¬ß4.4 scopes shrink-to-fit to "the epoch's qualified slate's total commitment"; a T13 branch-rerun seeds additional books mid-lifecycle whose subsidy is not part of any slate qualification. B10's shrink-to-fit freezes the slate commitment at Seed entry and does not re-charge reruns (status-quo: reruns proceed; their obligations still enter the treasury `pol_commitments` NAV netting). Should reruns instead be budget-charged (and refusable) against the current epoch's meter? | 08 ¬ß4.4/¬ß3; 05 ¬ß2.1 T13 | 2026-07-17 (B10 WP-B) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B4 ratification).** Ratified: a T13 rerun's extra POL is **not** charged against the current epoch's `pol.budget_epoch`. Shrink-to-fit is evaluated once over the qualified slate at Seed entry and a rerun is by construction not part of any qualification (`pallets/epoch/src/lib.rs:942-944`, `crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:856-914`). No second meter is needed because the exposure is **structurally** bounded rather than budget-bounded: `delayed_once` and `rerun` are one-way flags with mutually exclusive producers, so each proposal admits at most one rerun and one POL doubling, capping epoch rerun exposure at 2√ó the qualified commitment. 08 ¬ß4.4 gained the normative paragraph; the obligation still enters NAV netting and the seed fails status-quo if `POL` cannot fund it. |
| SQ-248 | **06 ¬ß7 challenge-bond basis: live `att.bond` or the challenged attestor's held bond?** "anyone posting a bond ‚â• 50% of the attestor bond" diverges whenever att.bond is amended between registration and challenge; the live reading makes contesting in-flight CODE/META artifacts more expensive after a raise (defense-softening direction). B10 remediation implements the conservative stored-bond basis (symmetric with the slash side). Ratify or flip to live. | 06 ¬ß7; R-7 | 2026-07-17 (B10 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** 06 ¬ß7 now states the **held-bond** basis normatively: the challenge floor is 50% of the bond held by the challenged attestor when the challenge opens, both slash sides use the same basis, rounding is up against the forfeiting party, and an `att.bond` amendment binds a member only at next seating; 13 ¬ß1's row carries the binds-at-seating note. The row's "symmetric with the slash side" justification was **verified, not assumed** ‚Äî both legs read `AttestorInfo.bond` (`crates/attestor-core/src/lib.rs:261-267` and `:316-322`), pinned by `pallets/attestor/src/tests.rs:404-497`. Verification also supplied the argument the row lacked: the live reading is defense-softening in **both** directions ‚Äî after a raise it prices contesting out, and after a cut it makes challenge-spam denial-of-attestation cheap, since an open challenge suppresses its attestation from quorum and `att.bond` is reachable to √ó0.5. One residual recorded, not resolved: a challenge open against an attestor who is then recalled becomes permanently unresolvable against them (`attestor-core:316-321` returns `NotMember`), so a for-cause recall shields the attestor from adjudication ‚Äî harmless only while bonds are arithmetic-only. |
| SQ-249 | **07 ¬ß6 in-flight binding of the oracle bond schedule and R_max.** Do `orc.bond_floor`/`orc.bond_bps`/`orc.rounds` bind per game at round-1 creation (B_r = frozen B_1¬∑2^(r‚àí1), ladder length fixed at open) or read live per escalation? Live reading lets a legal amendment retroactively under-collateralize an admitted metric (adversarial blocker) and breaks try-state on legally reachable states. B10 remediation freezes {B_1, R_max} per game at open. Ratify the freeze (07 ¬ß13's freezing language currently covers StakeAtRisk only). | 07 ¬ß6.1/¬ß13; 15 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-17 (B10 dual review, blocker-driven) | resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B3): **ratified.** 07 ¬ß6.1 gained a normative *Per-game freezing of `B_1` and `R_max`* paragraph ‚Äî both bind at round-1 creation, no escalation re-reads `orc.bond_floor`/`orc.bond_bps`/`orc.rounds`, so a META amendment prices only games opened after it; refusing to open a game whose full ladder is unrepresentable is normative too. ¬ß13's freezing line extended from escrow movement to parameter amendment; 13 ¬ß1's `orc.bond_bps` row restates it. Verified: `crates/oracle-core/src/lib.rs:502-512` (`StoredRoundSchedule`), `:716-721` (escalation reads stored), `:1287-1303` (doubling independent of live params), adversarial test `pallets/oracle/src/tests.rs:1503`. |
| SQ-250 | **`pallet_xcm.claim_assets` needs a 06 ¬ß3.3 authority-matrix row.** B10 classifies it Public (09 ¬ß6.1 self-scoped Signed reclaim requires it) and proves Treasury-origin dispatch for protocol-keyed traps; 06 ¬ß3.3 has no row (only `pallet_xcm.{send, force_*}` ‚Üí nobody). Add the row (Public, self-scoped by AssetTrap keying) under the S5 inventory discipline? | 06 ¬ß3.3; 09 ¬ß6.1 | 2026-07-17 (B10 spec-review) | open |
| SQ-251 | **Are protocol custody accounts metered under `phase3.dep_cap`?** Treasury-origin trap recovery deposits to the treasury protocol account are recorded in the per-account cumulative meter (test-pinned; status-quo-safe). Once cumulative recoveries exceed the 20,000 USDC default, further protocol recovery refuses until a phase gate raises the cap. Intended, or should protocol accounts be exempt (cf. the 08 ¬ß8 MaxPositions/deposit exemption precedent)? | 09 ¬ß5.2; 08 ¬ß8 | 2026-07-17 (B10 spec-review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**reclassified 2026-07-20**, B4 ratification ‚Äî refused; implementation owed). Metering confirmed with no exemption anywhere in `pallets/inflow-caps` (test-pinned at `runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/tests.rs:2857-2894`), and the cap is **cumulative-forever**: `CumulativeDeposits` has exactly one writer and no decrement, window reset or migration, and 09 ¬ß5.2 makes both keys raisable *only* by phase gates. So once protocol recoveries pass the 20,000 USDC default, protocol trap recovery is refused until a phase-gate upgrade ‚Äî no operator action can unblock it. Funds are re-trapped rather than destroyed, so this is a liveness trap, not a loss ‚Äî the same class as SQ-215/SQ-235. The precedent the row cites cuts against ratifying: 08 ¬ß8 exempts protocol accounts from `MaxPositionsPerAccount` and the position deposit for exactly this reason, and `phase3.dep_cap` is a D-13 per-*user* containment control, so metering the protocol's own custody under it is a category error. Fix = apply the 08 ¬ß8 exemption list to `pallet-inflow-caps`; sentence lands in 09 ¬ß5.2. |
| SQ-252 | **Split-gate scope: Signed ledger splits only, or also MarketAuthority set minting?** 09 ¬ß5.2 says caps are "enforced at every `split`/inflow-crediting path"; B10 gates the four Signed split extrinsics (the SQ-105 reading); the trade path's MarketAuthority splits stay exempt, so `buy` can create escrow while over cap (issuance base unaffected ‚Äî defense-in-depth question only). | 09 ¬ß5.2 | 2026-07-17 (B10 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** 09 ¬ß5.2 now scopes the split-path read to the four **Signed** ledger split extrinsics and states the `MarketAuthority` trade-path exemption with its reason. The row's central safety claim ("issuance base unaffected") was **independently verified rather than accepted**: a `buy`-path split escrows collateral the trader already holds ‚Äî `settle_collateral` only ever calls `Collateral::transfer`, never a mint (`pallets/conditional-ledger/src/lib.rs:1463-1487`) ‚Äî and every unit of that balance entered issuance through `CappedInflows::mint_asset`, the sole production issuance path, since `pallet_assets::mint` is classified `Nobody` for all origins including sudo and genesis seeds zero USDC. No unbacked claim, no cap bypass. One correction in the *unsafe* direction, recorded for a future row: the registry seeds both `phase3.*` keys with `min = 0`, `max = u128::MAX` and no monotonicity (`crates/constitution-core/src/lib.rs:1904-1923`), so 09 ¬ß5.2's "raised only by phase gates" is procedurally stated but **unenforced**. |
| SQ-253 | **Is the mint-step global cap defined over issuance-increasing mints only?** `TrapRecoveryInflows` bypasses `mint_admissible` for pallet-xcm's issuance-neutral trapped-imbalance reconstruction (deposit leg stays metered; over-cap recovery re-traps; safety test-pinned). 09 ¬ß5.2 is silent on the exemption. Pin "the mint-step gate binds issuance-increasing mints" in 09 ¬ß5.2 or require cap checks on reconstruction too. | 09 ¬ß5.2/¬ß6.1 | 2026-07-17 (B10 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** 09 ¬ß5.2 now pins the mint-step gate to **issuance-increasing** mints and states the trapped-imbalance reconstruction exemption. Issuance neutrality was **proven in the pinned SDK sources, not asserted**: the trap forgets the imbalance (`pallet-xcm-29.0.0` `src/lib.rs:3910`) and the claim mints a duplicate then drops it (`:3953-3968`), net zero ‚Äî so the trapped holding is already inside `total_issuance` and re-applying the cap would double-count. Refusing this row would have been the dangerous choice under R-7: the trap entry is consumed **before** `mint_asset` and the error arm only logs, so a cap check at reconstruction would silently destroy recoverable user funds whenever issuance sits near the cap. The deposit leg stays metered and double-claim is impossible (the trap counter is decremented before the mint). |
| SQ-254 | **Is a snapshot "overdue" while the dead-man pause blocks its epoch's close?** The schedule-derived deadline keeps running during a pause, but the paused clock prevents epoch close and `record_snapshot` legally requires epoch < CurrentEpoch ‚Äî an over-long pause would latch a snapshot cause no crank can clear, making recovery unreachable (no origin can clear bit 6). B10 remediation suppresses snapshot-overdue evaluation for epochs whose close is blocked by the active pause. Ratify. | 05 ¬ß4.8; 13 ¬ß2; 06 ¬ß3.3 | 2026-07-17 (B10 spec-review, major) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß4.8 gained a normative *snapshot-overdue under an active pause* paragraph ratifying the B10 suppression. The ruling states the reachability argument explicitly ‚Äî evaluating the trigger against an epoch whose close the pause itself blocks would latch a cause no crank can clear and no origin can reset ‚Äî and pins the suppression **narrowly**, matching the shipped predicate rather than the row's looser wording: it applies only while the clock is paused **and** the outstanding snapshot's due epoch is at or after the current index. An epoch that had already closed before the pause is **not** suppressed and may still re-arm the detector. Trigger 1 is unaffected. |
| SQ-255 | **Dead-man resume: 05 ¬ß4.8 "open decision windows extend day-for-day" vs 05 ¬ß5.4 step-2 ProcessHold and market window registration.** decide() terminally rejects in-flight proposals during engagement per ¬ß5.4's pseudocode (pre-existing A8 reading), and shifted `decide_at` schedules need their registered market decision windows re-registered to the shifted boundary or sealing wedges (adversarial major). B10 remediation extends the registered windows to the shifted boundary on resume. Which straddle semantics does ¬ß4.8 intend? | 05 ¬ß4.8/¬ß5.4; 04 ¬ß7 | 2026-07-17 (B10 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß4.8 gained a normative *straddle semantics on resume* paragraph: "open decision windows extend day-for-day" binds the **registered market decision window**, not merely `decide_at`. On resume the pause duration shifts `decide_at` (Trading/Extended) and `maturity`/`grace_end` (Queued/Suspended/FailedExecuted), and each affected proposal's registered window is **extended in place** to the shifted boundary ‚Äî code-verification corrected the row's wording here: the mechanism is an in-place end shift that refuses already-sealed windows, not a re-registration. The ¬ß5.4 tension is resolved by scope: step 2's `ProcessHold` governs proposals decided *while engaged*; the extension governs windows surviving to a post-resume decision. |

No production code needed. |

Recorded residual: `observe_proposal_terminal` hard-fails on an empty book set, unreachable today but a G-1-shaped coupling the rule now forbids. |

A claimant-adverse reading would ceil both. | 07 ¬ß6.1; R-7 | 2026-07-18 (B10 remediation) | resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B3): **ruled ceil both.** 07 ¬ß6.1 wrote `orc.bond_bps √ó StakeAtRisk` with neither a `/10,000` divisor nor a rounding direction ‚Äî both were unspecified, so this fixes them together: new *Units and rounding* paragraph makes the division and any parameter-representation conversion round **up**, resolved against the bonded party (a bond rounded down weakens exactly the deterrence ¬ß6 supplies); `max(¬∑, orc.bond_floor)` applied after rounding; ¬ß6.3 admission is bps-space and unaffected either way. 13 ¬ß1 restates it. | SQ-225 | **No normative SCALE decode-depth bound at the guard's preimage `Decode` boundary.** `MAX_NESTED_LEVELS = 4` (06 ¬ß3.3) is a *post-decode* filter bound; the guard's `decode_batch` (`RuntimeBatch<T>::decode` over preimage-sourced bytes) had no depth limit, so a hash-committed ‚â§`MAX_BYTES` preimage encoding one deeply-nested call recurses in `Decode` until the wasm stack-height trap (native: abort) ‚Äî a G-1/rule-1 violation in audit-scope-A code, surfaced by S2's decode-fuzz work and confirmed by both reviews. **S2 ruling (G-1 fail-closed):** added kernel `MAX_PAYLOAD_DECODE_DEPTH = 256` (substrate's conventional extrinsic decode-depth limit; ‚â´ the ‚â§4 semantic nesting, ‚â™ the stack budget) and switched `decode_batch` to `decode_all_with_depth_limit`; over-deep input ‚Üí `BadPreimage`. Runtime regression `deep_preimage_batch_decode_fails_closed_at_the_depth_limit`. Should 09 ¬ß1.2/¬ß2 pin the decode-depth invariant normatively (rather than leaving it an implementation detail)? | 09 ¬ß1.2; 06 ¬ß3.3; G-1 | 2026-07-17 (S2 dual review, blocker) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** The spec was genuinely silent ‚Äî no decode-depth bound appeared anywhere in 09, 13 ¬ß2 or 15 ¬ß4.5 ‚Äî so 09 ¬ß1.2(11) now pins it normatively (depth limit `MAX_PAYLOAD_DECODE_DEPTH`, mandatory full consumption of the committed bytes, `Rejected(BadPreimage)` on violation, and an explicit statement that `MAX_NESTED` is a *post-decode* bound that cannot bound the decode), with a new 13 ¬ß2 kernel row so the 15 ¬ß4.6 / I-22 limit-coverage extractor can see the constant. Verified before ratifying: **all four** preimage-fetch boundaries route through the single depth-limited `decode_batch` ‚Äî no second unguarded site ‚Äî and 256 is not an invention but substrate's own `MAX_EXTRINSIC_DEPTH`, used by the SDK's preimage realize path; SCALE depth accounting is effective for every `RuntimeCall` nesting form via `Box`/`Vec` `descend_ref`. |

Synced through RB-INTAKE frontmatter/body and the rule's `threshold` annotation. | SQ-241 | **02 ¬ß12 `ReleaseChannel` reserved flag bits under a future schema bump.** 02 ¬ß12 freezes bits 0‚Äì2 and says "others reserved zero", while allowing future fields to be appended beyond offset 168 with a schema bump. May bits 3‚Äì31 of `flags` ever become non-zero under `schema > 1`, or are they frozen zero forever? Decides whether frozen-layout readers must hard-reject (O5's current behavior ‚Äî isolated so only the ReleaseChannel metric family degrades) or mask reserved bits. | 02 ¬ß12; 12 ¬ß3.1 | 2026-07-18 (O5 spec review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; settled in doc 12 ‚Äî no 02 edit, no contract bump).** 02 ¬ß12 is already complete on the writer side (bits 0‚Äì2 fixed, bits 3‚Äì31 reserved zero, extension only by appending beyond offset 168 under a schema bump); what it never stated is **reader** behaviour on a violation, and reader obligations are doc 12's subject matter ‚Äî so the ruling lands in 12 ¬ß3.1 and `INTEGRATION_CONTRACT_VERSION` stays at v5. Substance: read together, those two 02 rules make the reserved bits **frozen zero for the life of the layout** ‚Äî a future flag is a new appended field, never a redefinition of bits 3‚Äì31 ‚Äî so a non-zero reserved bit is a record no conforming writer produced. Readers therefore **reject rather than mask** (masking would silently accept an unauthenticated writer's record), rejection MUST surface as degraded/unknown and never as healthy (for ¬ß3.2 neither "no newer release" nor "no security flag"; for ¬ß6.3 the series go absent, never zero), and readers MUST accept values **longer** than 168 bytes ‚Äî which the shipped O5 reader already does. Nuance recorded: the degraded family also includes `bleavit_chain_pending_upgrade_age_blocks`. |
| SQ-242 | **Minimum release-key signature count is unquantified.** 12 fixes ‚â• 2 *attestations* (¬ß1.4(2), ¬ß5.2(2)) but never fixes a floor for release-key *signatures* on a canonical release ("signing key IDs", plural, unquantified). The O5 attestation monitor refuses to default it: the operator config must state an explicit minimum. Should 12 (or a 13 row) fix a normative floor? | 12 ¬ß1.4, ¬ß2.1, ¬ß5.2 | 2026-07-18 (O5 spec review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; a floor is fixed rather than left to operators).** 12 ¬ß1.4 now fixes **‚â• 2 valid release-key signatures from distinct active keys of the current keyring generation**, counted after excluding keys revoked in `ReleaseChannel` ‚Äî the same floor as attestations, for the same reason: a canonical release must survive the loss or compromise of any single release key, and a one-signature release makes a single key a unilateral shipping authority, the concentration ¬ß2.2 exists to prevent. Deployments MAY require more, MUST still state their minimum explicitly, and MUST NOT go lower; the O5 monitor's floor moved from ‚â•1 to ‚â•2 while keeping its refusal to default. Two corrections to the row: "signing key IDs" is ¬ß1.1, not ¬ß2.1, and the monitor enforced an *explicit* minimum, never a *safe* one ‚Äî an operator could lawfully configure 1. |
| SQ-243 | **Two ¬ß6.3 table-2 rows lack a defined observable.** (a) "update missing 600 blocks after repoint": no on-chain observable marks the repoint block ‚Äî O5 anchors the lag at the first finalized head where the gateway-majority ArNS target diverges from `ReleaseChannel.manifest_txid`. (a) The repoint lag is anchored at the first finalized head where the gateway majority and `ReleaseChannel.manifest_txid` **disagree**, since no on-chain observable marks the repoint block; ratified with the detail the row omitted ‚Äî the clock also starts when the gateways reach *no* strict majority, deliberately, because an inconsistently resolvable name is not healthy. (b) The joint window is the **maximum** per-operator retention, valid precisely because every window ends at now; per-operator shortfalls above that floor belong to the bootnode program (¬ß6.2). Correction: the row says O5 "alerts on" it ‚Äî the rule exists but `bleavit_bootnode_served_state_retention_days` is an **O3 seam with no producer**, so `max()` over an empty vector cannot fire. The spec fixes the rule; O3 still owes the feed. `deploy/monitoring/README.md` corrected to say so. |

| SQ-271 | **Held-exposure self-certification of the depth certificate (mechanism round 2).** With SQ-231's contest-capital measure live, churn/wash can no longer inflate step-5 grading or step-9 `LÃÇ` ‚Äî yet the re-calibrated rates barely moved (PARAM 3.46 ‚Üí 3.81 %, TREASURY 1.52 ‚Üí 1.64 %): an attacker who *holds* net exposure through the window supplies genuine contest capital at the cost of market risk rather than 3P, so any aggregate depth measure the attacker can supply stays self-funding in thin organic environments. The sim's own evidence names the structural fix: CODE/META sit at 0.000 % **because gate books catch what the depth certificate cannot**. Candidate directions: (a) extend Survival/Security-style gate books to PARAM and TREASURY ‚â§ 1 % NAV (04 ¬ß1.1 inventory + POL cost impact); (b) an opposing-side/at-risk measure (likely the same aggregate self-funding wall); (c) accept-and-document (declined once). Blocks G0 criterion B and S4/V-12 publication. | 08 ¬ß5.2‚Äì¬ß5.3; 04 ¬ß1.1/¬ß7a; 05 ¬ß5.4 step 9; 14 TH-4; 15 ¬ß4.9 | 2026-07-18 (completion-drive re-calibration) | **resolved 2026-07-19 (user ruling; recorded 2026-07-20 in the triage sweep).** The at-risk depth measure (direction b/R1) was implemented and **reverted**: excluding balanced riskless inventory zeroed every false-pass but also broke honest formation (0 % adoption, 100 % false-reject), because the `v_min` floors and the `v_min = 2P` identity are calibrated on the gross measure ‚Äî switching it would need a whole-scale depth re-calibration tied to the unmeasurable A-2 real-depth split. The user ruled **R2 (use gates)**; V-12 published with 0 profitable sub-3P exploits and per-class decidable-harm false-pass < 1 %, and G0 reached `phase0_exit: true`. Direction (a) ‚Äî extending Survival/Security-style gate books to PARAM and TREASURY ‚Äî remains a Phase-3 economics option, not a blocker. |

Implemented across spec/runtime/reference/simulation and mutation-locked by an asymmetric differential vector. |
| SQ-274 | **The 09 ¬ß3.2 migration-failure control surface (`[VERIFY]`) does not exist, so the PB-MIGRATION drill cannot run.** Drill 06's `force-failure`/`retry` branches dispatch `migrations.force_failure`/`migrations.retry`, but upstream `pallet-migrations` exposes only `force_set_cursor`/`force_set_active_cursor`/`force_onboard_mbms`/`clear_historic` and B6 added no drill control calls ‚Äî and without a forcible failure the `MigrationHalt` trigger can never go live, so the guardian playbook activation correctly refuses `TriggerInactive` (first execution 2026-07-18 proved the whole 5-of-7 workflow up to that refusal). Options: (a) a bounded Root-only drill-control extrinsic in the runtime (R-7 concern: a production runtime carrying a force-failure surface); (b) re-spec the drill to stage a genuinely stalled MBM via `force_set_active_cursor` + B6's >900-block stall detector (SQ-132 bridge ‚Äî no new calls, but ~90 min of stall per run, **and** the SafetyFilter classifies the migration-admin calls as D-13 nobody rows ‚Äî `CallFiltered` bare and under every wrapper incl. sudo ‚Äî so (b) additionally needs an authorized staging route or a filter ruling; adversarial-review catch); (c) a non-release test-runtime feature (violates 15 ¬ß5 "against exactly those artifacts"). Drill 06 is re-gated on this SQ meanwhile (release-tier, so evidence stays fail-closed with attribution). | 09 ¬ß3.2; 06 ¬ß6.2 | 2026-07-18 (G1 first drill execution) | **RESOLVED 2026-07-20 (G1, decide-yourself ruling).** No production `force_failure`/`retry` surface ‚Äî option (a) rejected (R-7: the release runtime must not carry a fault-injection surface); a real stuck FRAME cursor trips the MBM lockdown that pauses the very guardian workflow the drill exercises, so option (b) is unsatisfiable (the B12-flagged tension); a test-runtime feature violates 15 ¬ß5, rejecting (c). Resolution: the drill stages the guard trigger `pallet_execution_guard::MigrationHalt=true` at genesis via a new benign `pallet-execution-guard` **`migration_halt` genesis field** (defaults false; drill spec stays PLAIN so the pinned zombienet schedules the para ‚Äî a raw storage-injected spec is not schedulable), decoupled from a live cursor so extrinsics flow and the guardian calls dispatch. **Passed end-to-end 2026-07-20 (3/3):** `assert-halt` verifies the staged trigger (storage-key self-checked); the guardian 5-of-7 recovery runs `propose_action` + four `approve_action`s and the dispatching approval **correctly fails closed** ‚Äî the runtime's `playbook_calls(Migration)` returns `DispatchError::Other("PB-MIGRATION cursor retry has no EmergencyPlaybook-safe runtime call")` (retrying a stuck cursor needs Root-only controls; fabricating Root would widen the EmergencyPlaybook origin, R-7). With the trigger ACTIVE this is `Other`, distinct from the pre-SQ-274 `TriggerInactive` (trigger never engaged) and from a successful activation (none exists ‚Äî the freeze arm is the automatic halt-source bridge, the retry/rollback the ratified expedited-CODE lane). The 09 ¬ß3.2 [VERIFY] is thereby confirmed: pallet-migrations' native controls are the (correctly D-13-filtered) surface; the `force-failure`/`retry` legs are undemonstrable-by-design and removed. Note SQ-278 (whether a no-effect Migration playbook approval should revert vs. record a FailedAction) |

| SQ-278 | **Should a guardian approval that reaches a no-dispatchable-effect playbook revert, or record a `FailedAction`?** Surfaced by drill 06's first real activation (SQ-274): the Migration playbook has no EmergencyPlaybook-safe runtime call (`playbook_calls(Migration)` returns `DispatchError::Other`), so with the trigger active the dispatching 5-of-7 approval **reverts the whole extrinsic** rather than recording a `FailedAction` and succeeding. Reverting is fail-closed (no effect dispatched, no origin widening ‚Äî R-7) and is what drill 06 now asserts, but it means a legitimately-5-of-7-approved Migration playbook can never reach an activated/recorded state through the guardian path (its freeze arm is the automatic halt-source bridge; its retry/rollback is the ratified expedited-CODE lane). Confirm the revert semantics, or should the pallet record a `FailedAction` for accountability on a no-effect activation? | 06 ¬ß6.2; 09 ¬ß3.2 | 2026-07-20 (G1 drill 06 first activation) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B2 ¬∑ ratified).** The revert stands as fail-closed (no effect dispatched, no origin widening ‚Äî R-7/G-1), but ratifying it required fixing the sentence it contradicted: 06 ¬ß6.2's "**Every** activation emits `PlaybookActivated`, consumes the allowance, writes a review record" was simply **false for one of its own six rows**. ¬ß6.2 now scopes that quantifier to effect-dispatching activations and adds the no-effect rule ‚Äî a playbook row with an empty admissible call set has no dispatchable activation, records nothing, and its accountability path is the automatic `MigrationHalt` bridge and that bridge's event stream, not the guardian review. Verified: PB-MIGRATION is the **only** such row (the other two `playbook_calls` error arms are argument validation already rejected earlier by `BadPlaybookTarget`), and the failing path is production-reachable, not hypothetical. The row's proposed remedy was **not** adopted: a `FailedAction` record already exists in `pallets/guardian` with a different meaning ‚Äî the review-deadline-miss recall substrate ‚Äî so reusing the name would have made 5 approvers directly recall-eligible via `guardian.recall` for a no-effect activation. |
