Ties SQ-274/SQ-278. | 06 ¬ß6.2; 09 ¬ß3.2 | 2026-07-20 (G1 spec-reviewer) | **RECONCILED 2026-07-20 (G1, spec-reviewer-verified).** 06 ¬ß6.2's PB-MIGRATION admissible-call-set cell + 09 ¬ß3.2 amended: no guardian-dispatchable call on stable2606 (cursor controls Root-only, D-13 "nobody"); freeze = automatic `MigrationHalt` execution-queue bridge; retry/rollback = expedited-CODE lane. Matches the runtime's `playbook_calls(Migration)` fail-closed `Err`. |
| SQ-281 | **09 ¬ß3.2(1)'s ledger/market inflow-freeze mechanism is unspecified for the no-cursor case (spec-reviewer minor).** ¬ß3.2(1) says the playbook freezes the execution queue AND ledger/market inflows, but the runtime's `MigrationHalt` gates only the execution queue; `playbook_calls(Migration)` constructs no `ledger/market.set_frozen`, so a genuine cursor relies on the FRAME MBM lockdown for the inflow freeze and drill 06's source-less staged halt freezes no inflows. Is the MBM lockdown accepted as the ¬ß3.2(1) inflow-freeze mechanism (making the explicit ledger/market freeze wording redundant), or must `MigrationHalt` independently gate ledger/market inflows for the no-cursor case? | 09 ¬ß3.2 | 2026-07-20 (G1 spec-reviewer) | **RECONCILED 2026-07-20 (G1, spec-reviewer-verified).** 09 ¬ß3.2(1) ruled: for a genuine cursor the FRAME MBM lockdown (`MultiBlockMigrator = Migrations`) IS the ledger/market inflow-freeze; `MigrationHalt` is the automatic execution-queue halt-source bridge. A source-less staged halt (SQ-274 drill) has no half-migrated layout, so it gates the execution queue only ‚Äî no separate guardian-dispatchable ledger/market freeze exists. |
| SQ-282 | **Drill-04 dead-man does not engage on a relay *finality* stall ‚Äî the `observe_dead_man` relay-parent-**jump** detector vs 05 ¬ß4.8's "no parachain block finalizes" (first-execution finding).** Drill 04's first-ever run (on the SQ-128 fast-timing runtime, `DEAD_MAN_RELAY_BLOCKS` compressed 4,800‚Üí48) reached the 48-relay-block stall in ~8 min, but `assert-dead-man engaged` **failed** ("dead-man did not pause the epoch clock and execution queue"). Root cause is a detector/scenario mismatch, **not** timing: the parachain kept producing throughout the stall (liveness `dead-man-pre-fault,2` passed), so `data.relay_parent_number` advanced smoothly and `observe_dead_man` (`pallets/epoch/src/lib.rs:1521`) saw no ‚â•-threshold jump. The B10 detector fires on a relay-**parent** jump between consecutive parachain blocks (a parachain **outage / catch-up** signal), whereas a relay-**finality** stall keeps the relay producing and the parachain follows the unfinalized best ‚Äî exactly the open 05 ¬ß4.8 `F`-signal `[VERIFY]` ("exact accessible validation-data fields on stable2606"): `PersistedValidationData` exposes `relay_parent_number` but not the relay's finalized-head number, so the runtime cannot compute best-over-finalized on-chain. Options: (a) the drill induces a parachain outage (pause **collators**, not relay validators) so a genuine relay-parent jump fires the detector ‚Äî but that tests catch-up, not finality; (b) extend the detector with a relay finalized-head input **if** the relay state proof exposes it (audit-scope-A, closes the 05 ¬ß4.8 [VERIFY]); (c) accept the detector as an outage/catch-up backstop and re-scope 05 ¬ß4.8's "no parachain block finalizes" wording. The `DEAD_MAN 4,800‚Üí48` compression is proven correct and independent of this. | 05 ¬ß4.8 (`F` [VERIFY]); 09 ¬ß7.1; `pallets/epoch` `observe_dead_man` | 2026-07-20 (G1 drill-04 first execution) | **RESOLVED 2026-07-20 (G1, decide-yourself ruling; spec-reviewer-verified).** SDK verification (three checks over the stable2606 pin) is definitive: the parachain runtime **cannot** observe the relay GRANDPA finalized head ‚Äî `PersistedValidationData` carries only `parent_head`/`relay_parent_number`/`relay_parent_storage_root`/`max_pov_size`, no `well_known_keys` finality key exists, and `cumulus_pallet_parachain_system` surfaces only the relay best/anchor. So option (b) (a finalized-head detector input) is impossible on the pin; a pure relay *finality* stall is not on-chain-detectable. Resolution = (c)+(a): the 05 ¬ß4.6 `F`-signal `[VERIFY]` is closed (accessible field = `relay_parent_number`; `F` is inclusion/liveness, not GRANDPA-finality ‚Äî the detector code was already `relay_parent_gap`-based and correct), 05 ¬ß4.8 trigger 1 + 13 ¬ß2 + 14 TH-37 re-scoped to the observable relay-parent-gap semantics with the finality-stall residual honestly assigned to an off-chain monitor (SQ-283) + XCM-isolation bound, and **drill 04 re-pointed** to a real relay-parent-gap collator outage (pause all collators ‚Üí relay best advances ‚â• 48 ‚Üí catch-up jump engages the on-chain dead-man). First execution proved the mechanism (bit 6 + `incident_active` latch, relay parent 108); the acute queue-freeze is transient under the compressed catch-up so the drill asserts the durable engaged flag (the acute freeze stays unit-tested, `pallets/epoch` `dead_man_pauses_phase_and_rejects_submission`). Spec-review: 0 blocker; the finality-lag-alert pointer defect is SQ-283 |
| SQ-283 | **The SQ-282 dead-man re-scope assigns a pure relay GRANDPA *finality* stall (relay best advances, GRANDPA lags, parachain keeps building on the unfinalized best) to an off-chain relay best-over-finalized monitor, but 12 ¬ß6.3 has no such alert.** The only Epoch-progress alert (`bleavit_chain_tick_lag_blocks > 600`) reads the *parachain* finalized head and would freeze (never fire) during a relay finality stall, so it does not cover the SQ-282 blind spot the spec now points at. Add a relay best-over-finalized-lag alert to 12 ¬ß6.3 + the O5 monitoring stack (exporter + `series-inventory.toml` + the `check_alert_coverage.py` gate), or ratify that the finality-stall residual is accepted as bounded by XCM isolation (I-24) + the relay's own GRANDPA finality-gadget security with no Bleavit-side detection. The on-chain re-scope (SQ-282) is complete and verified; this is only the off-chain layer. | 05 ¬ß4.8; 14 TH-37; 12 ¬ß6.3 | 2026-07-20 (G1 spec-reviewer, SQ-282 follow-up) | open ‚Äî owner O5 monitoring |
| SQ-284 | **Raw `futarchyTreasury.state` (the rolling-meter `TreasuryState`) does not SCALE-round-trip under portable metadata (polkadot-js): a read returns ~9,358 stored bytes that re-encode to ~17,678 (first surfaced by drill 05's raw-state read).** The runtime round-trips its own `TreasuryState` (audit-scope-A; unit-tested encode/decode + try-state), and the 02 contract routes every consumer through `FutarchyApi` views, never raw treasury storage ‚Äî so this is a portable-metadata/polkadot-js decode quirk on the rolling-meter type, not a runtime-correctness or solvency defect. Confirm the 02-views-only reading (raw-storage decodability is outside the contract surface), or investigate the specific field (likely a `RollingMeter` bucket vector) that trips the polkadot-js decoder for indexer/tooling friendliness. Drill 05's probe was changed to dispatch + assert `RenewalWindowClosed` only (no raw-state read). | 02 ¬ß4; 08 ¬ß2; `pallet-futarchy-treasury` `TreasuryState` | 2026-07-20 (G1 drill-05 first execution) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî **the contract question is answered: the 02-views-only reading is confirmed.** 02 ¬ß7 now states that storage items it does not list are not contract surface and that raw decodability of unlisted items under portable metadata is not guaranteed; every treasury consumer binds `nav()`, and raw `futarchyTreasury.state` is not in ¬ß7. The decode observation itself is recorded as **unreproduced**: the repository holds no captured bytes, portable metadata, polkadot-js version or decoder trace to confirm the 9,358 ‚Üí 17,678 figures or localize a `RollingMeter` field, so it is closed as a non-contract tooling observation rather than carried as a phantom defect. Honest residual, deliberately not minted as a new row: drill 05's `stage` and `execute` branches still read raw `State`, so if the quirk is real it can still surface there. |
| SQ-285 | Should the dead-man **recovery epoch expose an exit for intake bonds**? Shipped behaviour closes `withdraw` for the whole recovery epoch: the legal guard is `proposal.epoch == CurrentEpoch`, recovery is a fresh index (resume increments the clock) and `submit` is refused throughout, so no proposal can satisfy it ‚Äî a proposer whose bond is held cannot exit for ~1 epoch. Bounded deferral, not forfeiture: the bond stays held and withdrawal reopens when carry-forward re-anchors the proposal on the roll ending recovery. But G-1 argues an exit that only *releases* funds and advances nothing should not be gated on a liveness incident the proposer did not cause. Options: (a) ratify the deferral as-is; (b) relax `withdraw`'s epoch guard to accept any `Submitted` proposal while recovery is set, cancelling and refunding immediately; (c) re-anchor carried-forward proposals at the *start* of recovery rather than at the roll that ends it, which restores the guard without touching `withdraw`. (b) and (c) are code; (c) interacts with the intake-queue admission bound. | 05 ¬ß4.8 | 2026-07-20 (B1 ¬∑ Codex P2 review of PR #114) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (ratified as shipped; delegated ruling).** Option (a): the deferral stands. 05 ¬ß4.8 now states it normatively. The reasoning that closed it is that the G-1 test was misapplied when this row was raised ‚Äî G-1 constrains what the protocol *does* on a failure path, and declining to process an exit is **inaction**: no bond is forfeited, no claim is created, the proposer's position is preserved exactly, and withdrawal reopens on the roll that ends recovery. Relaxing `withdraw`'s epoch guard would be the *active* choice ‚Äî releasing funds and mutating intake bookkeeping while the epoch clock is paused and carry-forward re-anchoring is mid-flight is more state movement during an incident, not less. Option (c) (re-anchoring carried-forward proposals at the *start* of recovery) was rejected for the same reason plus its interaction with the intake-queue admission bound. No code change. |
| SQ-286 | Should an active `PB-LEDGER-FREEZE` **force-reject `Queued` proposals, or let them wait the freeze out**? SQ-98 fixed the *disposition* (tick and decide now agree: T20 `ProcessHold` + par void) but deliberately left the *swept set* as shipped, which includes `Queued` and `FailedExecuted`. Two spec texts pull the other way: 06 ¬ß6.3 scopes the effect to "decision windows overlapping a freeze", which is a trading-side notion that does not obviously reach an already-adopted mandate; and 09 ¬ß1.2(10) gates `execute()` on "no active PB-LEDGER-FREEZE", which reads as *queued items wait* ‚Äî a gate that is near-vacuous if the sweep destroys them first (it survives only as a same-block belt-and-braces check). G-1 cuts both ways: force-rejecting is status-quo in the sense that nothing executes, but it also destroys a legitimately adopted decision because of an unrelated ledger emergency, and the freeze is bounded (‚â§ 14 d + one renewal) so waiting is feasible if grace windows are extended. A coherent reading exists ‚Äî sweep the trading-side states (their books are frozen, so they genuinely cannot produce decision-grade signal) and let execution-side `Queued` items wait ‚Äî but it needs a grace/maturity extension mechanism for freezes that does not exist today (the dead-man has one; a freeze does not). **Second, adjacent, sub-question found while fixing SQ-98:** the freeze sweep in `tick` sits *before* the dead-man/recovery no-op (`crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:1456` vs `:1475`), so a freeze coinciding with a dead-man pause or a recovery epoch force-rejects live proposals ‚Äî contradicting both that block's own comment ("in each case tick is a no-op ... never decided or force-rejected") and the SQ-90 ratification that recovery holds transitions. With SQ-98's fix `decide` refuses with `BadPhase` during recovery while `tick` still destroys, so the outcome converges on tick rather than diverging, but the precedence between the two emergency regimes is unstated in 05 ¬ß4.8 and 06 ¬ß6.3 and should be ruled with the scope question. | 06 ¬ß6.3; 09 ¬ß1.2(10); 05 ¬ß2.1 T20 | 2026-07-20 (SQ-98 fix) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (ratified as shipped; delegated ruling).** Both halves ruled, both normative in 06 ¬ß6.3 now. **Sweep scope:** the freeze sweep covers every live non-terminal state, `Queued` included. Force-rejection *is* the status quo (the payload does not dispatch either way), and letting a mandate wait would need maturity/grace extension machinery that exists only for the dead-man (05 ¬ß4.8), not for a freeze ‚Äî without it a waiting mandate just expires mid-freeze, the same terminal outcome reached later and with a worse bond disposition. 09 ¬ß1.2(10) is **not** left vacuous: the keeper sweep is per-proposal and bounded, so between activation and the sweep reaching a given mandate there is a real window in which `execute()` must refuse. **Precedence:** the freeze wins over a concurrent dead-man pause. The two answer different failures ‚Äî the dead-man is a *liveness* pause that preserves state, `PB-LEDGER-FREEZE` a machine-checked *solvency* anomaly (I-4 drift) in which backing is in doubt ‚Äî and a stalled clock is not a reason to keep live proposals standing over a possibly-unbacked ledger. This is the shipped ordering (`crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs`: the sweep precedes the dead-man hold), so the code comment added with SQ-98 is now backed by spec text. No code change. |
| SQ-287 | **Decide-time preimage mismatch slashes 10 %, but 06 ¬ß4(5) says constitution violations slash 100 %.** 05 ¬ß1.3 names `ConstitutionViolation` as the decide-time producer for a preimage mismatch; 06 ¬ß4(5) reads "100 % slash on constitution violation or false resource declaration"; but `crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:1094` applies `intake_slash_pct` (10 %, `:123`) to the whole `r == NotDecisionGrade \|\| !guards.preimage_ok` arm, so a decide-time preimage mismatch forfeits 10 %, not 100 %. Which is right is genuinely open. 06 ¬ß4(2) already carves preimage-missing *cancellation* (T4, screening) out at 10 %, and the B1 SQ-191 ruling held that 100 % is reserved for a **verified culpable act** ‚Äî while ¬ß7.3 preimage pinning at qualification is specifically designed to make proposer-side unnoting structurally impossible, which argues a decide-time mismatch is an infrastructure failure rather than proposer fault and should slash 10 % or nothing. Options: (a) amend 06 ¬ß4(5) to scope 100 % to screening-stage verified violations and name the decide-time mismatch as a 10 % row; (b) raise the code to 100 % for the `!preimage_ok` arm; (c) refund entirely on G-1 grounds. **Pre-existing** ‚Äî predates and is independent of the SQ-98 fix, which only changes *whether this arm is reached* during a ledger freeze. | 06 ¬ß4(5); 06 ¬ß4(2); 05 ¬ß1.3 | 2026-07-20 (SQ-98 adversarial review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (ratified as shipped; delegated ruling).** The code is right and 06 ¬ß4(5) was over-broad; ¬ß4 gained a normative scoping paragraph. The 100 % slash covers **screening-stage (T4) *verified*** findings only ‚Äî a verified constitution violation or verified false resource declaration. A **decide-time preimage mismatch** terminates `Rejected(ConstitutionViolation)` (05 ¬ß1.3) but carries rule 2's **10 %**, which is what `epoch-core` applies. Two reasons, the same one twice: confiscation requires a verified culpable act (the B1 SQ-191 taxonomy ‚Äî every implementation-unverifiable static failure cancels refundably, and rule 2 already prices the preimage family at 10 %); and decisively, 09 ¬ß7.3 **pins the preimage at qualification** precisely so the proposer cannot unnote it, making the sabotage window structurally empty ‚Äî so a mismatch by decide time is an infrastructure or third-party failure, and slashing it in full would punish the one party the pinning design establishes is not responsible. The reason code stays `ConstitutionViolation` (it names *what* failed, not who is culpable); only the disposition is pinned. No code change. |
| SQ-288 | **03 ¬ß7 R-4 requires the ledger sovereign, treasury sub-accounts and book accounts to be "genesis-endowed and can never be reaped" ‚Äî no genesis path endows any of them with USDC.** `runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/genesis.rs` registers the USDC and DOT assets and their metadata but sets `accounts: vec![]`, and `deploy/genesis/allocations.template.json` carries no USDC endowments either. Consequence: `pallet_conditional_ledger::settle_collateral` uses `Preservation::Preserve`, so the **last full redeemer of the last open vault** fails with `Token(NotExpendable)` once the sovereign's balance would fall below USDC `min_balance` ‚Äî a redemption that is legal under 03 ¬ß5.3 becomes impossible, stranding the final claimant. Reach is narrow (requires system-wide escrow to drain to zero, so it is a quiet-chain/wind-down condition rather than a steady-state one) but the failure mode is exactly the R-4 clause's purpose. Options: (a) seed the three account classes at genesis per R-4, which also needs the allocations template and the chain-spec validator to carry them; (b) switch the settlement path to `Preservation::Expendable` and let the sovereign be reaped, which contradicts "can never be reaped"; (c) amend R-4 if the endowment is genuinely unnecessary. Found while building the SQ-92 runtime fixtures ‚Äî those fixtures mint exactly `minimum_balance(usdc_location())` to the ledger account with an R-4 citation, so the gap is worked around in tests, not in production. | 03 ¬ß7 R-4; 03 ¬ß5.3 | 2026-07-20 (SQ-92 test authoring) | **closed 2026-07-20 ‚Äî reclassified out of *Spec questions* as milestone B14.** This was mis-filed: it is not a question. 03 ¬ß7 R-4 is unambiguous ("ledger sovereign, treasury sub-accounts and book accounts are genesis-endowed and can never be reaped") and the code simply does not implement it, so there is nothing to rule ‚Äî only work to schedule. Tracked as **B14** with the full failure analysis; the one genuine open sub-question (whether R-4 can even bind *book* accounts, which are created dynamically at Seed rather than at genesis) is carried in that milestone's scope rather than as a standing SQ row. |
| SQ-298 | **Three treasury budget lines carry normative budgets with no consumer implementation.** Surfaced by SQ-52's verification pass: `ORACLE` is called a "per-epoch line" in 08 ¬ß1.1 but nothing meters it; `REWARDS` carries the 08 ¬ß1.1 proposer-reward schedule (PARAM 500; TREASURY/CODE min(0.05%¬∑Ask, 25k); META 25k, "paid on `Executed`") with **no payment code anywhere** in `runtime/` or `pallets/`; and `collator.comp` (2,000 USDC/collator/epoch ‚Äî a frozen D-15 default, *not* `[VERIFY]`-gated) is a dead key despite 08 ¬ß2.4 mandating payment at epoch Housekeeping pro-rata to authored-block share. Unlike the eight `[VERIFY]`-gated `ops.*` rows these are concrete obligations with no owning milestone. Assign an owner, or doc-true 08 ¬ß1.1/¬ß2.4 to defer them explicitly. | 08 ¬ß1.1, ¬ß2.4; 13 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-20 (B4 ratification, SQ-52 verification) | open ‚Äî batch X |
| SQ-299 | **04 ¬ß7a's `q_pol_branch` description does not match the shipped mechanism.** ¬ß7a defines the POL exclusion as subtracting "the recorded protocol-seeded position (¬ß10 ‚Äî the same storage the 'POL undisturbed' check reads)", but `crates/market-core/src/lib.rs:421-425` excludes POL **structurally** ‚Äî seeding never touches `q_long`/`q_short`, so `q_pol_branch` is identically zero and no storage is read ‚Äî while the POL-undisturbed check separately keys on `SeededMarkets` and `book.b` (`pallets/market/src/lib.rs:1333-1339`). | SQ-300 | **08 ¬ß5.3's prize-scaled `pol.b` and Œ¥ are normative but unimplemented.** ¬ß5.3 mandates `pol.b(class, P) = b_floor(class)¬∑max(1, P/P_ref(class))` and the matching Œ¥ slope, and ¬ß5.3's ceiling non-bindingness argument depends on that seeding ("a book seeded at floor `b` while `v_min` carries the `2P` scaling is **not** a configuration the normative seeding produces"). | SQ-301 | **08 ¬ß5.4(a) contradicts ¬ß5.3's scaling rule.** The normative CODE worked example takes `P = 693,147` against `P_ref(CODE) = 341,589` ‚Äî a ratio of ‚âà2.03 ‚Äî yet seeds the **unscaled** floor `b = 60,000` for its POL-depth term (`2 √ó 60,000 √ó ln 2 = 83,178`); ¬ß5.3 requires `b = 60,000 √ó 2.03 ‚âà 121,760`, giving depth ‚âà168,800. ¬ß5.4(b) already carries a "Normative-seeding form" companion showing both readings, so (a) is the unfixed twin. | SQ-302 | **The settlement observer can permanently block a terminal transition it ought to tolerate.** 04 ¬ß2 and 03 ¬ß5.4 (B4 ratification, *corrected during the R-6 review*) require marker and latch to commit or roll back together ‚Äî the shipped `with_storage_layer` composition is right, and the batch's first draft had this backwards. The residual is narrower: `observe_proposal_terminal` hard-fails on `ensure!(!ids.is_empty(), TryStateViolation)` (`pallets/market/src/lib.rs:1655`), so a vault with no registered books would roll back its own `void`/`settle_scalar` on every attempt and never settle. Unreachable today ‚Äî vault creation and first book creation are the same call (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/configs.rs:2072-2091`) ‚Äî and the rollback direction is the safe one, so this is latent liveness, not a solvency risk. Make the empty-book-set case a legitimate no-op rather than an error, or establish the unreachability structurally instead of incidentally. | 03 ¬ß5.4; 04 ¬ß2; G-1 | 2026-07-20 (B4 R-6 review) | open ‚Äî batch X |
| SQ-303 | **`RederiveBudgets` is a normative screening obligation with no implementation, and B4 made it the sole safety rider for the frozen NAV floors.** 13 ¬ß5 item 6 says the classifier tags the trigger keys with a `RederiveBudgets` obligation "checked at screening"; the identifier occurs nowhere in `crates/`, `pallets/`, `runtime/` or `tools/` (the similarly-named `rederive_call` is the unrelated execution-guard payload re-derivation). B4 extended the trigger set from 4 keys to 10 and made 08 ¬ß4.1's *Freeze and re-derivation* paragraph lean on it, so it now stands between a one-META `pol.budget_epoch` cut and a ¬ß4.2 gate arming below the true floor. | SQ-307 | **The epoch-VOID Baseline settlement writes a terminal marker without latching, permanently wedging the Baseline book.** Found by applying B4's newly-ratified 04 ¬ß2 / 03 ¬ß5.4 reap-ordering rule to `d77b6f5` (SQ-92's fix). Every other terminal path composes marker + latch atomically ‚Äî `configs.rs:3452-3455` (`void`), `:3695-3702` (`settle_scalar`), `:3721-3730` (`settle_baseline` ‚Üí `observe_baseline_terminal`). The VOID path does not: `configs.rs:3415-3416` delegates straight to `pallet_welfare::settle_baseline_void`, which calls `T::Ledger::settle_baseline` (`pallets/welfare/src/lib.rs:736-745`) with **no** `with_storage_layer` and **no** `observe_baseline_terminal` ‚Äî and that observer has exactly one production call site (`configs.rs:3730`), which this path never reaches. Consequences under the ratified rule: `pol_obligation_live` stays true forever, `reap` (which requires the latch) can never fire, and the Baseline book's POL commitment stays a live NAV obligation ‚Äî one per voided epoch, monotonically accumulating ‚Äî while the ledger sweep deletes the marker after `ledger.archive_delay`, so nothing can reconstruct it. SQ-92 closed a stranded-funds leak and opened a standing-obligation one. Fix: compose the VOID path like the other three. | 03 ¬ß5.4; 04 ¬ß2; 08 ¬ß1.2 | 2026-07-20 (B4 R-6 review, applying the ratified rule to newly-merged main) | open ‚Äî batch X |
| SQ-120 | 08 ¬ß6.3 "100% ‚áí `KeeperBudgetExhausted`‚Ä¶ rebates stop": a literal 100% trigger never fires unless spend lands exactly on the budget (budget rarely an exact multiple of `keeper.rebate`). B9 implements **effective exhaustion** ‚Äî the first unpayable attempt emits the event (degenerately `{spent: 0}` when `rebate > budget` on a fresh meter, e.g. after a governance shrink) ‚Äî **plus a payment latch**: once emitted, rebates stay off for the rest of the epoch even if a mid-epoch `keeper.rebate` reduction would fit again. Ratify both readings. | 08 ¬ß6.3 | 2026-07-16 (B9 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B4 ratification).** Both readings ratified in a new 08 ¬ß6.3 bullet. Effective exhaustion (first non-fitting rebate attempt emits, reporting actually-spent ‚Äî degenerately 0) and the per-epoch **payment latch** (no further metered rebate after it fires, even if a later amendment would create headroom) are both normative, with `KeeperBudgetLow` always preceding. Verified not a liveness trap: the rebate is an infallible post-effect that never alters its crank, every crank stays permissionless and dispatchable at ordinary fee, and the shipped keeper daemon has no meter awareness at all. The latch's only real effect is that a mid-epoch budget *increase* cannot reopen payments ‚Äî the no-extra-outflow direction. |
| SQ-121 | 07 ¬ß4 mandates a "keeper-class fee rebate" for watchtower `ack_observed`; B9 pays it from the **oracle budget line** (`CrankClass::OracleLine`, consistent with SQ-116's routing) in **both** the oracle pallet and the registry instances ‚Äî though 07 ¬ß7's registry call table does not restate the rebate for the registry's ack. Confirm the routing and the registry-instance applicability. | 07 ¬ß4, ¬ß7 | 2026-07-16 (B9 dual review) | resolved 2026-07-20 (batch B3): **ratified, and the gap was wider than the row stated** ‚Äî 07 ¬ß7's call table carried **no** registry `ack_observed` row at all, and ¬ß4's rebate sentence is written against the oracle signature `ack_observed(component, epoch, round, report_hash)` while the registry's call is `ack_observed(epoch, filing_id)`, so "¬ß7 does not restate the rebate" understated it. ¬ß7 now carries the call row plus a normative *Watchtower acknowledgment and its rebate* note: same keeper-class rebate, paid from the oracle budget line, with the differing exposures ratified as deliberate ‚Äî the registry stops paying at `wt.quorum` acks per filing, an oracle round rebates one ack per registered watchtower up to `wt.max`. Both dedup per acknowledger and degrade fail-soft (ack succeeds, rebate simply unpaid) when the line cannot cover. Verified: `pallets/registry/src/lib.rs:552-580`, `pallets/oracle/src/lib.rs:664-684` (both `CrankClass::OracleLine`), `crates/futarchy-treasury-core/src/lib.rs:575-589` (fail-soft). ¬ß7's error list gained `NotRegistered`/`DuplicateAck`/`AlreadyQuorum`. |
| SQ-122 | 05 ¬ß4.7 is silent on `record_daily_gate` cardinality: the extrinsic is repeat-tolerant (a duplicate `(epoch, day, spec_version)` OR-merges identical flags ‚Äî a state no-op). B9 keeps it repeat-tolerant but rebates only recordings that actually change state (first write of the day, or genuinely new flags), closing the general-tranche drain both reviews flagged. Should duplicates instead be rejected outright on-chain? | 05 ¬ß4.7; 08 ¬ß6.3; R-7 | 2026-07-16 (B9 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B4 ratification).** Repeat-tolerance ratified; duplicates MUST NOT be rejected. 05 ¬ß4.7 gained a normative *Recording cardinality* paragraph: flags OR-merge so a duplicate `(epoch, day, spec_version)` is a **state** no-op, late/duplicate submissions from independent keepers are the expected operating mode, and rejecting them would convert a benign race into a failed dispatch the losing keeper must special-case. Rebate eligibility stays state-change-gated so duplicates cannot drain the meter, and the sample marker is required to stay distinct from the breach `day_bitmap`. |
| SQ-123 | Treasury custody sync: `fund_budget_line` moves value only inside the internal line ledger, while B9's rebate payouts transfer real USDC from the `KEEPER__`/`ORACLE__` `bl/trsry` sub-account pots ‚Äî nothing moves custody when a line is funded, so line-ledger vs pot balances can drift (funded line + empty pot = every payout silently skipped; funded pot + empty line = stranded funds). B9 adds the loud alarm (try-state: per payout line, internal balance ‚â§ pot balance) but NOT the sync mechanism ‚Äî which is the A9 "asset custody / fungibles" follow-up. Where does custody sync live (fund_budget_line moving USDC between sub-accounts, or a genesis/ops funding convention)? | 08 ¬ß1.1, ¬ß6; A9 follow-ups | 2026-07-16 (B9 Codex adversarial) | **resolved 2026-07-17 (B4-residual)** ‚Äî 08 ¬ß1.4 amended: `fund_budget_line` is custody-synced for the pot-backed lines (KEEPER/ORACLE) via the new `PotFunding` seam ‚Äî atomic MAIN‚Üípot real-USDC transfer, `Err` rolls back the internal credit; other lines keep custody in MAIN (A9 scope). Try-state drift alarm retained as backstop. |
| SQ-124 | 12 ¬ß6.3's keeper inactivity row ("no crank 1 h") doesn't say whether the signal is chain-observed (rebate/extrinsic events) or daemon-side, nor how to avoid false positives when a role legitimately has no due work for >1 h (no trading books d18‚Äìd21; capability-disabled roles on the current runtime). The reference keeper ships per-role metrics + last-success timestamps and the README recommends a `for: 1h` guard excluding disabled roles. Pin the intended semantics at the next 12 touch. | 12 ¬ß6.3 | 2026-07-16 (B9 off-chain spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; ratified as shipped, two claims corrected).** 12 ¬ß6.3 now fixes the reading normatively: the signal is **daemon-side**, evaluated per role against that role's last-successful-crank timestamp, deliberately not inferred from chain activity (which cannot tell an idle keeper from a role with no due work). The row's `for: 1h` recommendation is **not** what ships and was doubly wrong ‚Äî `for:` is a Prometheus rule field, not an Alertmanager one, and the shipped rule uses no `for:` at all, filtering instead on `timestamp > 0 and planned_total > 0`. The spec ratifies the shipped filter, requires per-role instantiation so a cleanup role cannot mask a decision-critical one, and **declares both blind spots** the filter creates ‚Äî a role stuck since process start (same zero timestamp as a disabled role) and, caught by the adversarial review, a keeper whose chain connection is down, which the liveness conjunct silences even though its cranks are equally not happening. `keeper/README.md` corrected to match. |
| SQ-125 | 13 ¬ß3.1 mandates that clients bind the phase fractions via pallet metadata constants, but `pallet-epoch` exposes no phase-fraction constants (`extra_constants` has only the contract version/bounds/`TickBatch`) ‚Äî so neither the keeper nor the future FE has a compliant binding path and both must mirror the kernel grid in code. Confirm the queued B2 contract amendment adds the actual metadata-constant exposure to `pallet-epoch`. | 13 ¬ß3.1; 02 (B2 amendment queue) | 2026-07-16 (B9 off-chain spec-review) | open ‚Äî keeper mirrors the grid with a documented note until the constants exist |
| SQ-126 | No spec text defines the off-chain keeper's parameter-sourcing obligation: must it track live `Constitution.Params` values (`mkt.obs_interval`, `dec.window`, `res.probe_*`), or may it ship normative defaults with operator overrides (as the reference implementation does, dynamic `ParamValue` decoding being unspecified for clients)? | 01 ¬ß4.2; 12 ¬ß6.1; 13 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-16 (B9 off-chain spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; resolved by adding text, not by citing it).** Verification refuted the row's premise that 01 ¬ß4.2 / 12 ¬ß6.1 already carry the obligation ‚Äî both are role/funding tables and say nothing about parameter sourcing, so the shipped behaviour was simply unspecified. 12 ¬ß6.1 now states it: live `Constitution.Params` tracking is **mandatory** for every 13 row governing a crank's due-time or admissibility, under the shipped precedence **override > live > documented default**, with compiled-in defaults required to equal their 13 genesis value. Rows with no safe default are **live-or-nothing** (the keeper skips rather than guesses) ‚Äî the shipped instance is `ops.ct_quote_ttl`, so it is five keys, not the four the row lists. The honest limit is recorded too: a keeper cannot distinguish "row absent" from "read failed", so both fall back identically and staleness is detected through keeper liveness series, not the chain. |
| SQ-135 | 15 ¬ß4.5 demands "a clean `cargo-audit` pass under the 01 ¬ß9 exact-pin regime", but the pinned stable2603 tree carries 24 RustSec vulnerabilities (16√ó `wasmtime 35.0.0`, `hickory-proto`, `rustls-webpki 0.101.7`, `ring 0.16.20`, `tracing-subscriber =0.3.19` ‚Äî the last pinned by `sc-tracing 45.0.0` itself) with **no fix available inside the pin regime**. B8 implements the only workable mechanism: per-entry-annotated `.cargo/audit.toml` ignores (each names the pinning SDK component + the obligation to clear it at the next stable2603 maintenance bump), keeper workspace audited with no inherited exceptions, and the full waived-ID list disclosed in every release manifest. Ratify this waiver mechanism (or amend 15 ¬ß4.5 to name it); the standing obligation to re-triage at every SDK bump stands regardless.. **Re-triaged 2026-07-17** against the 25 open Dependabot alerts: the waiver list is *exactly* precise (1:1 with the 23 firing ids ‚Äî zero stale, zero missing) and every entry is still pin-forced, with `cargo update --precise` attempted per family and the verbatim blocker recorded. Two refinements: (1) `lru` RUSTSEC-2026-0002 correctly needs **no** exception (`informational = "unsound"` ‚Üí an allowed warning), so the gate is green rather than under-configured; (2) the mechanism had a **structural hole** ‚Äî RustSec is a strict subset of the GitHub Advisory DB for crates.io, so `yamux` GHSA-vxx9-2994-q338 (HIGH, remote panic) was invisible to it entirely; closed by the GHSA-only leg under **SQ-219**. The *"remove at the next stable2603 maintenance bump"* obligation every entry carries now has a named owner: **B11** ‚Äî **executed 2026-07-17 (D-19 stable2606 line move)**: from-scratch re-triage deleted the 16 `wasmtime` entries; the 7 survivors + the yamux GHSA entry were each re-proven pin-forced on the new lockfile and re-annotated under per-family exit criteria (libp2p-forced / litep2p-forced / sc-tracing-forced) | 15 ¬ß4.5; 01 ¬ß9 | 2026-07-17 (B8; re-triaged by B11) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; waiver mechanism ratified into 15 ¬ß4.5).** The tension is real and now stated: the exact-pin regime and a clean advisory gate genuinely conflict when a pinned SDK component forces a vulnerable version, so "clean" means clean **under a declared, annotated exception set** subject to five normative conditions ‚Äî per-entry annotation naming the forcing component *and* a per-family exit criterion, recorded pin-forced proof, mechanical expiry plus from-scratch re-triage at every train bump, blast-radius containment for workspaces outside the SDK closure, and disclosure in every **publishable** release manifest. Waiver count is explicitly never a gate. Deliberately **source-agnostic**: which advisory databases are normative stays open as SQ-219 (batch B6), so this ruling cannot pre-empt it. Verified against the tree (7 annotated ignores + 1 GHSA waiver, keeper audited from its own root, `bleavit.supply-chain.v2` carried in the manifest); one nuance recorded rather than ratified ‚Äî keeper isolation rests on cargo-audit's CWD config discovery, contained by the version pin, not by a structural guarantee. |
| SQ-136 | 15 ¬ß5 requires artifacts "published ‚Ä¶ and mirrored content-addressed (Arweave)" before a release is publishable, but CI holds no keys (12/O1) ‚Äî the ordering is circular if "mirrored" must precede publication. B8 implements a two-step: the tag release is created **prerelease** with notes stating it is not canonical until an operator attaches mirror evidence (JSON mapping each SHA-256 ‚Üí Arweave TXID; documented in `tools/release/README.md`); the manifest carries `mirror.status: pending`. Open: who records the TXIDs, where the evidence lives on-chain/off-chain, and whether "publishable" in 15 ¬ß5 means the prerelease or the post-mirror promotion (O1 interplay). | 15 ¬ß5; 12 ¬ß1; O1 | 2026-07-17 (B8) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; ordering fixed in 15 ¬ß5).** The circularity was real because ¬ß5's "published ‚Ä¶ and mirrored" is a conjunction that never fixed an order. It does now: (a) CI publishes the artifact set as a **prerelease** whose notes state it is not canonical and whose manifest records the mirror duty outstanding; (b) an operator attaches mirror evidence mapping every content digest to its Arweave TXID; (c) the release is promoted to canonical. **"Publishable" in the gating rule is step (a)** ‚Äî what a runtime release must clear to ship at all ‚Äî while canonical status, which frontend releases and ¬ß1.4 repoints depend on, requires (c). The mutable-host independence ¬ß5 asserts is therefore preserved by (c), not by (a). Ratified as shipped: the pipeline creates a visible prerelease and every manifest carries `mirror.status: pending` unconditionally. |
| SQ-137 | What does a chainHead fixture "covering every event" (02 ¬ß11, 15 ¬ß5(4)) require on a fresh dev chain with no protocol activity? B8 records metadata presence per frozen 02 ¬ß6 variant plus real `System.Events` bytes at the pinned block on both transports; actually *emitting* every frozen event needs scripted scenarios that are impossible before A8/A11/B7. Pin the intended semantics (presence + system-event bytes now, scenario-driven emission once B7's suites exist?). | 02 ¬ß11; 15 ¬ß5(4) | 2026-07-17 (B8 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; semantics fixed in 15 ¬ß5 ‚Äî and the row's own reading corrected).** "Covering every event" is now defined per critical-surface entry as (i) metadata presence under the frozen name and (ii) rendered-layout agreement with 02 ¬ß11, plus **once per fixture** (iii) a real executed block's `System.Events` blob retrievable and non-empty over **both** the chainHead and legacy transports. Requirement (iii) is explicitly a single transport-and-liveness proof and **not** per-variant evidence ‚Äî the row's "plus real `System.Events` bytes" phrasing would have over-ratified: all 90 event entries record the *same* blob at the *same* pinned block, so the fixture never asserts any variant was emitted. Scenario-driven emission is the ¬ß4.7 suites' job, evidenced through the environment-run contract (SQ-139/SQ-202). |
| SQ-138 | 02 ¬ß9 mandates metadata-constant representations for the kernel floors/bounds but never freezes the constant *names* or owning pallets ‚Äî the B8 surface manifest had to invent names (`MaxIntakeQueue`, floor/bound constant identifiers) that descriptor/fixture gating will key on. Should 02 enumerate the frozen constant names the way ¬ß7 freezes storage names? Routes to the queued B2 02-amendment (with SQ-2/SQ-23/SQ-125). | 02 ¬ß9; 02 ¬ß13 | 2026-07-17 (B8 spec-review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C) ‚Äî **already resolved by contract v4; the row was stale.** 02 ¬ß9 carries the "Frozen metadata-constant names (SQ-138)" table, including `Epoch.MaxIntakeQueue`, and declares external-tool placeholders non-normative. Its *completeness* gap is SQ-217, resolved separately in this batch. |
| SQ-139 | B8 defined the environment run-evidence contract B7 must satisfy (`bleavit.env-evidence.v1`: per-file SHA-256 inventory, release wasm hash, release commit, named passing suites ‚Äî validated byte-for-byte at strict assembly so placeholder env dirs can never pass). Ratify the schema, and decide whether commit-level CI green satisfies 15 ¬ß5's "the backend's own suites ran against exactly those artifacts" or the tag pipeline must re-execute the Zombienet/Chopsticks suites against the packaged artifacts. | 15 ¬ß5; 15 ¬ß4.7 | 2026-07-17 (B8) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; schema ratified and the consumer hardened to match).** 15 ¬ß5 now names the frozen fields ‚Äî schema id, suite kind, release wasm hash, release commit, **tier**, named suites with results, per-file digest inventory ‚Äî requires assembly to validate them **byte-for-byte** against the packaged set and to refuse evidence not binding to the release under assembly, and permits additive producer fields that consumers MUST NOT depend on. Ruling on the row's second half: commit-level CI green does **not** satisfy 15 ¬ß5, because it proves the source was tested and never that the packaged bytes were; the tag pipeline re-executes against the built artifacts. Two gaps found and closed rather than ratified: `tier` was produced but unchecked, so a `g1`-stamped file satisfied the consumer; and ‚Äî caught by the adversarial review, which **empirically disproved** the first draft's claim that byte-for-byte validation stops a placeholder directory ‚Äî `artifact_hashes: {}` passed vacuously, because set-equality against an empty directory is trivially satisfied. `validate_run_evidence` now requires `tier == "release"` and a **non-empty inventory** (both tested; the inventory guard mutation-checked). Honest residual: evidence emission is still blocked fail-closed by SQ-203/SQ-204, so the contract is normative but unexercised, and the spec text is written as the contract rather than as present-tense behaviour. |
| SQ-202 | Tiered subsetting of 15 ¬ß5's gating rule: 15 ¬ß4.7 names keeper-loss, dead-man, and the XCM reserve-transfer suites among "the backend's own suites", but 04 (~8 h real finality stall), 08 (43,200 real relay blocks ‚âà 3 days), 09 (63-day soak, SQ-128) are physically infeasible per tag, and 03/07 are gated on unwired surface (B9 topology; SQ-101/B4). The B7 producer encodes a release/g1 tier split (validator-pinned: 04/05/08/09 = g1) and refuses evidence while any release-tier suite remains gated ‚Äî which suite subset satisfies "ran against exactly those artifacts"? Extends SQ-139. | 15 ¬ß4.7; 15 ¬ß5 | 2026-07-17 (B7 evidence producer, dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; tiering ratified as an explicit, bounded narrowing).** 15 ¬ß5 now admits a declared **release tier** against which the gating rule is satisfied, with the remainder in a **phase-gate tier** proven against 09 ¬ß7.1 ‚Äî and says plainly that this *narrows* the gating sentence, since ¬ß4.7 names the dead-man and XCM reserve-transfer suites among the backend's own suites and the release tier does not carry all of them. Admissible only under four conditions, all matching shipped behaviour: manifest-declared membership pinned by the environment validator (04/05/08/09 = g1); a release-tier suite that does not run and pass **blocks evidence emission** and never degrades to a pass; every exclusion recorded inside the evidence with its reason ‚Äî promoted to a **frozen, assembly-validated field** after the adversarial review caught that condition (3) otherwise elevated exactly the class of field the neighbouring paragraph forbids consumers to depend on; and evidence admissible only when stamped with the release tier (newly enforced ‚Äî see SQ-139). Membership is a claim about execution cost, never importance. Noted: G1 passing drills 04/05 did **not** re-tier them, and `--include-gated` remains the documented operator override. |

Promoting those two would cost ‚âà 6.2 USDC/epoch against a ‚â• 9,600 USDC reservation; left as General (the G-1 direction) and flagged as an available refinement. |

Conservative for the reserved tranche but under-rebates the decision-critical observation load (the 20% general cap starves observation rebates at scale). Bind with the A8 runtime wiring. | 08 ¬ß6.3; 04 ¬ß7; 05 ¬ß2 | 2026-07-16 (B9) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (triage sweep ‚Äî code-verified).** The pending always-false adapter is gone: `RuntimeInDecisionWindow` is a real `Contains<MarketId>` reader bound at `type InDecisionWindow` (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/configs.rs:2712-2713, 2767`), so decision-window observations rebate against the reserved ‚â•80 % tranche as 08 ¬ß6.3 intends rather than starving in the General cap. |
| SQ-109 | **Ratify two B3 genesis-economics rulings** made under the session's standing delegation (both conservative, both spec-pinned in the amended 08 ¬ß2.1): (a) founding-team vesting = the **0%-at-cliff** single-schedule curve (not the conventional 25% catch-up ‚Äî see the 2026-07-16 Decision-log row for why catch-up is unsafe at genesis); (b) **unvested VIT cannot pay transaction fees** (strict "nothing spendable before the cliff"; founding-team accounts pay fees in USDC per 08 ¬ß9 ‚Äî a reviewed-then-removed fee carve-out adapter is preserved in git history if the ruling is reversed). | 08 ¬ß2.1, ¬ß9; D-15 | 2026-07-16 (B3 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B5 ratification; both rulings ratified, one mechanism corrected).** (a) Ratified as shipped: the 0%-at-cliff single schedule is in `genesis.rs` (`begin = BLOCKS_PER_YEAR`, `liquid = 0`), pinned by `team_vesting_curve_is_cliffed_and_never_faster_than_the_ideal_curve`, and enforced on the production side by the allocations template plus the validator's exact-equality `TEAM_VESTING_SCHEDULE` pin. (b) Ratified in outcome, **refuted in mechanism** ‚Äî the row credits a strict lock, but `UnvestedFundsAllowedWithdrawReasons = TRANSACTION_PAYMENT` literally *permits* fee payment; the strictness is emergent from `FungibleAdapter`, whose `fungible` API has no withdraw-reason concept. 08 ¬ß2.1 now binds the **adapter** (no fee path may exempt a vesting lock; the exemption set is inert and is not authorization), so an adapter swap is a spec violation rather than a silent regression; the runtime test driving the real fee path is the standing guard. The row's "preserved in git history" claim is also inaccurate: pickaxe over all refs finds no adapter code ‚Äî only the `7354d5c` commit message records the decision. |

| SQ-106 | 02 ¬ß3/¬ß4 spell the API collection bounds as FRAME's `BoundedVec<T, ConstU32<N>>`, but 02 ¬ß2 + rule 9 / 01 ¬ß5.2 require every view type to live in the frame-free `futarchy-primitives`, whose `BoundedVec<T, const N: u32>` cannot take the sp type ‚Äî the spec's spelling and the crate-layering rule are in literal tension. Residual: the **TypeInfo/metadata shape** differs (derived composite vs FRAME's sequence-like info), which generated frontend descriptors will surface ‚Äî B1a's `impl_runtime_apis!` golden-metadata test must decide whether to keep the composite shape (and bless it in 02 ¬ß3's wording) or hand-implement a sequence `TypeInfo`. | 02 ¬ß3‚Äì¬ß4; 01 ¬ß5.2; rule 9 | 2026-07-16 (B2 spec-review + Codex adversarial) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî 02 ¬ß3/¬ß4 now spell every API collection `futarchy_primitives::BoundedVec<T, N>` and freeze the shipped composite metadata form and type path. **The row's load-bearing residual is false:** FRAME's `bounded_collections::BoundedVec` renders in the real runtime metadata as a *composite* wrapper around `Vec<T>` exactly like the primitives type ‚Äî only the type path differs, so there is no composite-versus-sequence decision to make and no hand-written `TypeInfo` is needed. Wire equality was already pinned both directions. |

| SQ-103 | **(bot P1) Values referenda track collapse + track-config code home.** Stock `pallet-referenda` selects the track from the proposal origin (`track_for`), so the five 06 ¬ß2.1 tracks that all produce `ConstitutionalValues` (metric/constitution/entrenched/guardian/ratify) cannot be discriminated ‚Äî collapsing them onto one track ID would let an entrenched-scope action (e.g. lowering the entrenched-class `att.bond`) enact at a weaker track's threshold. **B1a fix:** the single shared `ConstitutionalValues` track now uses the **strongest (entrenched) thresholds** ‚Äî 80% approval, 20%‚Üí10% support, 50,000-VIT deposit, 4-epoch-equivalent (7 d/28 d/7 d, 28 d enactment) ‚Äî so **no values action can enact below its required bar** (over-strict but G-1-safe); `OracleResolution` keeps its own track (60% / 10%‚Üí3% / 7 d). Residual: per-direction floor discrimination (tighten vs loosen) and the SQ-150 amend_registry contest |

Safe only pre-genesis ‚Äî the SDK forbids this counter ever decreasing after genesis. **Two row claims corrected:** it is stale at "=3" (the contract was at 5), and `transaction_version` denotes *dispatchable compatibility* embedded in signed-transaction validity, not the extrinsic **format** version. |

| SQ-110 | 09 ¬ß5.2's "excess inbound reserve transfers fail politely, funds remain on Asset Hub" is only partially realizable at the XCM layer. B4 enforces the **global** cap at the *mint* leg (`ReserveAssetDeposited` ‚Üí `mint_asset`), so an over-`tvl_cap` transfer fails on its first instruction with nothing minted and nothing trapped locally ‚Äî but the sender's USDC has already moved into our sovereign account *on Asset Hub* by then (the reserve leg runs there first), so "remains on Asset Hub" means "sits in our AH sovereign account pending recovery", not "stays with the sender". The **per-account** cap is only checkable at the deposit leg (beneficiary unknown at mint), where a rejection traps the minted holding ‚Äî recoverable per the amended 09 ¬ß6.1 trapped-assets row. Is trap-plus-self-scoped-recovery (+ FE pre-check, 11) the accepted "polite" failure? | 09 ¬ß5.2; 09 ¬ß6.1 | 2026-07-16 (B4 dual review) | **resolved 2026-07-17** ‚Äî amended 09 ¬ß5.2 codifies mint-leg refusal plus deposit-leg trap/self-scoped recovery; `pallet-inflow-caps` now owns the Phase-3 per-account meter and `PhaseInflowCaps` is ready for `CappedInflows`. FE pre-check remains Track F; production transactor composition waits on SQ-101. |

B4's seam guidance compares **total local USDC issuance** (strictly tighter, status-quo-safe: issuance ‚äá ledger+treasury holdings). Under the literal base no user deposit could ever breach the cap at the inflow leg ‚Äî which would make 09 ¬ß5.2's "enforced ‚Ä¶ at the XCM inflow leg" vacuous. Confirm the issuance base (and amend 09 ¬ß5.2's wording) or define what the inflow leg should compare. | 09 ¬ß5.2 | 2026-07-16 (B4 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-17** ‚Äî amended 09 ¬ß5.2 uses total local USDC issuance; the runtime adapter reads real `ForeignAssets` issuance and `pallet-inflow-caps::mint_admissible` enforces the live constitution cap without writes. |

B4 ships the `TraderRates` seam; B1a cannot bind it to `pallet-constitution::Params` until 13 ¬ß1 owns keys/defaults/bounds for them. Which keys (e.g. `xcm.trade_dot_ref`/`xcm.trade_dot_pov`/`xcm.trade_usdc_ref`/`xcm.trade_usdc_pov`), what defaults, what class? | 09 ¬ß6.1; 13 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-16 (B4 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-17** ‚Äî 13 ¬ß1 owns `xcm.dot_per_sec`/`xcm.dot_per_mb`/`xcm.usdc_per_sec`/`xcm.usdc_per_mb` with exact tested metadata; the runtime's `ConstitutionTraderRates` reads `Params` live and backs the two-dimensional `GovernedWeightTrader`. |

Is send/fail/probe-timeout-only the accepted v1 resolution (with `R` alone driving the daily C flag when X is partial, as ¬ß6.4's fail-static fallback already provides)? If yes the `[VERIFY]` can be closed at the next 09 touch. | 09 ¬ß6.4; 07 ¬ß8; 05 ¬ß4.3 | 2026-07-16 (B4 review) | **resolved 2026-07-17 (B4-residual)** ‚Äî 09 ¬ß6.4's `[VERIFY]` closed: v1 X = locally-accepted sends √∑ (accepted + local failures + probe timeouts), partial by construction, R fallback load-bearing; welfare `XcmTraffic` counters + `HealthTrackingRouter` wrap + oracle `ProbeTimeoutSink` + the `MetricInputs` X emission are live. R itself stays deliberately unbound (SQ-195). |
| SQ-127 | PB-MIGRATION checkpoint lifetime: 09 ¬ß3.2(2) makes `pre_upgrade_checkpoint` the audit anchor *during* a halt ‚Äî which begins after the upgrade applied, i.e. after 09 ¬ß2.1 has the guard clear `PendingUpgrade`; but the implemented guard invariant (A11 try-state) pairs `PendingUpgrade`/`PendingUpgradeCheckpoint` both-Some/both-None. Does the checkpoint survive the clear during a halted migration? The B7 pb-migration fixture injects the consistent paired form; the halted-migration-shaped image is undecidable until answered. | 09 ¬ß3.2(2); 09 ¬ß2.1 | 2026-07-16 (B7 dual review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**RULED 2026-07-20**, batch B2, jointly with SQ-144; implementation owed). Premise confirmed, and the refusal understated it in one place and misattributed it in another. *Understated:* the anchor is missing at **both** homes ‚Äî nothing writes `QueuedExecution.pre_upgrade_checkpoint` at `execute()` either, because the core deletes the queue row and discards the value behind a comment claiming an `UpgradeAuthorized` event home that 02 ¬ß6's frozen two-field shape does not provide (`crates/execution-guard-core/src/lib.rs:532-545`). *Misattributed:* clearing the field at enqueue is **not** the defect ‚Äî 09 ¬ß1.1 says it is "written at `execute()`", so the enqueue clear is conformant sanitization. **Ruled** in 09 ¬ß3.2(2): capture at code *application* (guard `on_initialize`, first block after the swap, where `parent_hash` is the last pre-migration block and nothing has stepped), anchor `(block_number, block_hash)`, single-homed in a dedicated guard item, try-state relaxed from equality to implication-plus-latch. **No contract bump** ‚Äî the item appears nowhere in 02. Original refusal: The row poses a dichotomy (checkpoint survives ‚áí try-state fails, or checkpoint cleared ‚áí anchor lost); reality is a **third state** that is worse than either. Both items are killed atomically at the relay GoAhead callback ‚Äî `PendingUpgradeCheckpoint::kill()` sits beside the `PendingUpgrade` clear (`pallets/execution-guard/src/lib.rs:1464`) ‚Äî which runs *before* any multi-block migration can step, so try-state passes **and** the anchor is gone, making 09 ¬ß3.2(2) vacuous rather than violated. The spec's primary home is never populated either: `QueuedExecution.pre_upgrade_checkpoint` is forced `None` at enqueue (`lib.rs:942`) and the row is deleted on success, while the secondary home is impossible because 02 ¬ß6 freezes `UpgradeAuthorized` without a checkpoint field. Net: an ordinary post-upgrade migration failure has **no on-chain audit anchor**, and `deploy/runbooks/RB-UPGRADE.md:44-49` already instructs operators to read those empty cells. Must be resolved jointly with SQ-144 by moving capture to application time. **Implementation owed (jointly with SQ-144):** latch where `PendingUpgradeCheckpoint::kill()` sits today, capture `(block_number, block_hash)` in the guard's `on_initialize` on the first block after the code swap, and single-home it in a dedicated guard item; delete `RuntimeDispatcher::checkpoint()`, the `BatchDispatcher::checkpoint` trait method and its mock, the `UpgradeAuthorization.state_root` field and the `sp_io::storage::root` call; relax try-state to the one-way rule of 09 ¬ß3.2(2). Riders: align the core's three-field `UpgradeAuthorized` to 02 ¬ß6's frozen two-field spelling, and restore an upgrade-payload `prime_execute` so the `execute` benchmark exercises the CODE branch again (B1b switched it to PARAM). Re-point RB-UPGRADE step 3 at the real item. |
| SQ-128 | 09 ¬ß7.1 Phase-1 exit "Zombienet: 3 unattended epochs": at the 13 ¬ß1 default `epoch.length` = 302,400 blocks that is a ~63-day real-time soak (907,200 blocks); the only uncompressed route to floor-length 201,600-block epochs is an on-chain META decision after boot. Drill 09 targets 3 √ó the release default and is labeled the G1 long-soak. Is a genesis `Params` override to the 201,600 floor acceptable for the *local drill* profile (it is a legal 13 ¬ß1 value), or must the drill run at the release default? | 09 ¬ß7.1; 13 ¬ß1 `epoch.length` | 2026-07-16 (B7 spec review) | **RULED 2026-07-20 (G1, user-selected).** The 63-day literal soak is both infeasible and redundant ‚Äî G2 (Phase-2 Paseo exit) already requires ‚â•6 real epochs on a live Paseo testnet, which IS the release-cadence unattended-epochs evidence. Neither knob compresses it while running the exact release artifact: `epoch.length`'s kernel floor (`MIN_EPOCH_LENGTH_BLOCKS = 201,600`) is still ~42 days at 6 s, and the 6 s Aura slot cannot realistically go sub-second (Bleavit is fully block-denominated, R-2). **Resolution (split):** the G1 local drill 09 runs a **compressed machinery proof** via a feature-gated fast-timing test runtime (small epoch length + proportionally scaled kernel timing constants; real Aura+relay consensus; ~minutes; a documented test-only build, not the release binary) proving the epoch machine cranks 3 cycles unattended; the **release-cadence "3 unattended epochs" is subsumed by G2's ‚â•6-epoch Paseo run**. **IMPLEMENTED 2026-07-20 (G1).** A two-Explore-agent coupling audit showed the empty-progression drill needs a far smaller set than the ~15-constant pre-estimate: the default-off `fast-timing` cargo feature gates only two kernel floors (`MIN_EPOCH_LENGTH_BLOCKS`, `DECISION_WINDOW_FLOOR_BLOCKS`) and three `constitution-core` registry seeds (`epoch.length`/`dec.window`/`dec.trailing`), all derived from one knob `kernel::FAST_DAY_BLOCKS = 4` ‚áí `epoch.length = 84` (21 √ó FAST_DAY); forwarded `futarchy-primitives` ‚Üí `constitution-core` ‚Üí `pallet-constitution` ‚Üí `bleavit-runtime`. `BLOCKS_PER_DAY` and every emergency/execution/oracle/dead-man window stay at production values ‚Äî they cannot fire inside a 252-block drill, and `attack_cost_hat`/`t_dec` is never invoked with no proposals, so D-4 security economics are untouched. Each `cfg(not(fast-timing))` arm is textually identical to the frozen 13 ¬ß1 value; release-invariance is proven under default features by the `production_epoch_timing_floors_are_frozen` guard test, the unchanged `genesis_registry_matches_13_1_row_encodings` byte-assert + genesis-keys fixture, and the fact that `cargo test --workspace` never enables the feature. The wasm builds into a separate `target/fast-timing` dir (`generate-relay-specs.sh` ‚Üí `bleavit-drill-fast.json` + `zombienet/networks/bleavit-fast.toml`); `chain-spec-builder --verify` re-runs `EpochParams::validate` over the compressed genesis. Drill 09 boots it and asserts three real epochs (`epoch.epochOf().index ‚â• 3`, `epoch.schedule().length` read live) under genuine Aura+relay consensus with the keeper cranking `Epoch.tick`. Release-cadence "3 unattended epochs" stays subsumed by G2's ‚â•6-epoch Paseo run. |
| SQ-129 | 15 ¬ß4.7 requires the XCM suite to cover "trap, and recovery", but the amended 09 ¬ß6.1 trapped-assets row permits self-scoped `claim_assets` only for *self-keyed* traps; an inbound AH-message failure keys the trap under Asset Hub's origin, recoverable only by an inbound `ClaimAsset` program from that origin. Which route must the published drill exercise? | 15 ¬ß4.7; 09 ¬ß6.1 | 2026-07-16 (B7 dual review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**RULED 2026-07-20**, batch B2, under delegated authority; implementation owed). Implementation scoping confirmed the ruled mechanism is feasible on the pinned stack via a barrier-side pre-scan, which sees the full instruction slice, the origin and runtime storage, and on rejection returns before the executor VM is constructed ‚Äî nothing minted, nothing trapped. It also confirmed the rejected mechanism (2) is not merely wrong but **impossible**: XCM v5 exposes no fallible pre-mint hook carrying the beneficiary (`can_check_in` is teleport-only and takes the origin), which is exactly why ¬ß5.2 now says the beneficiary is unknowable *through the `AssetTransactor` interface*, never through the message. **One honest correction to the ruling's framing:** the fix does not make an over-cap depositor's funds *recoverable* ‚Äî they sit in this chain's Asset Hub sovereign account, as ¬ß5.2 already accepts for `tvl_cap`. What it buys is no local issuance inflation, no orphaned trap entry, no try-state-visible orphan, and semantics symmetric with the global cap. Original refusal and ruling: **refused as scoped, then RULED 2026-07-20 under delegated authority ("decide on your own what is best for the protocol"); spec amended, implementation owed.** The routing question is already answered by the code (drill 07 exercises the remote AH-keyed trap and an AH-side `ClaimAsset` recovery), but ratifying that would close the row while a live contradiction *inside 09* stays open: **09 ¬ß5.2 promises the sender a self-scoped `claim_assets` recovery for a `deposit_cap` rejection, and 09 ¬ß6.1 plus the runtime prove it impossible** ‚Äî the trap is keyed under Asset Hub (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/tests.rs:2654` asserts a Signed account cannot claim it), and ¬ß6.1's stated upstream mitigation (mint-leg enforcement) structurally cannot cover `deposit_cap`, which ¬ß5.2 itself says is enforceable only *after* the mint. Consequence: an ordinary Phase-3 depositor who exceeds their 20,000 USDC per-account cap has the funds trapped and **permanently unrecoverable from this chain's side** ‚Äî `pallet_xcm.send`/`execute` are `Nobody` for every origin including sudo, so only a Polkadot/AH governance referendum can release them. **Ruling: mechanism (1) ‚Äî enforce both caps before any local mint, so a cap refusal never creates a local trap.** 09 ¬ß5.2 now requires the per-account check to bind the beneficiary **extracted from the inbound program** (`DepositAsset{beneficiary}`); ¬ß5.2's premise that the beneficiary is unknowable pre-mint is true only of the `AssetTransactor` interface, never of the message, which carries it. ¬ß6.1's mint-leg mitigation is restated to cover both caps. **Mechanism (2) was rejected on evidence, not taste:** re-keying the trap to the beneficiary via `SetAssetClaimer` cannot work for a *cumulative per-account* cap, because a recovery claim's deposit leg stays metered ‚Äî `TrapRecoveryInflows::deposit_asset` delegates straight to `CappedInflows` (`runtime/bleavit-xcm/src/caps.rs:308-316`, pinned by `production_xcm_signed_claim_reconstructs_at_the_global_cap_and_records_deposit`) ‚Äî by the very cap the beneficiary already exceeded, and that cap never clears within the phase, so the holding would re-trap on every attempt. It converts unrecoverable funds into unrecoverable funds with extra steps. **Mechanism (3) was rejected** as documenting a fund-stranding bug as intent, contradicting ¬ß6.1's own amendment rationale (which exists precisely to avoid permanently stranded funds). **Residual (X):** the bleavit-xcm implementation ‚Äî beneficiary extraction at the barrier or a pre-execution scan, with the per-account check moved ahead of the mint ‚Äî plus a regression proving an over-cap inbound deposit mints nothing and traps nothing. Until that lands the stranding path is live in code, which is why this row stays open. Also unexecuted: drill 07's recovery leg has never run (pinned-Paseo AH inherent trap, `tools/env/suites.json:59-64`). **Implementation owed:** a `DenyOverCapInflows` barrier component walking the inbound program under the existing bounded-instruction budget ‚Äî sum USDC across `ReserveAssetDeposited`/`WithdrawAsset`/`ClaimAsset`, extract each `DepositAsset { beneficiary }`, convert with the same `LocationToAccountId` the deposit leg uses, and refuse when either cap would break, before the executor VM is constructed. Needs a pure-read `usdc_inflow_admissible` seam on `InflowCaps` (the barrier must never write); `CappedInflows::deposit_asset` stays the transactional writer as defence in depth. Beneficiary-conversion failure must **fall through** rather than refuse, or drill 07 ‚Äî whose trap is an unconvertible `PalletInstance(255)`, not a cap breach ‚Äî stops trapping. Moves the I-22 lexical binding for `phase3.dep_cap` (a barrier rejection surfaces as `XcmError::Barrier`), so the registry row and the test marker must move in the same commit or the gate goes red. |
| SQ-130 | Frozen-event drift (pre-existing): 09 ¬ß1.5/¬ß2.1(3) reference `UpgradeApplied` and an `applicable_at` field on `UpgradeAuthorized`, but 02 ¬ß6's frozen guard event list carries neither. The A11 pallet emits `UpgradeApplied` (the B7 drills assert it). Reconcile at the next 02 amendment batch (contract-version bump rules apply). **B6-merge extension (2026-07-17, PR #65 bot P1):** the new append-only `UpgradeAborted { code_hash }` (SQ-131 abort ruling) joins the same reconciliation batch ‚Äî like `UpgradeApplied` it is emitted-but-uncontracted until 02 ¬ß6 is amended with the batch's version bump + joint sign-off; the FE meanwhile observes abort state via the ReleaseChannel raw key, which the abort clear updates. | 09 ¬ß1.5/¬ß2.1; 02 ¬ß6 | 2026-07-16 (B7 spec review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî folded into SQ-95's single guard-event append: `UpgradeApplied { code_hash, spec_version }` and `UpgradeAborted { code_hash }` are now frozen in 02 ¬ß6. **The `applicable_at` half of the row is stale and was refused:** 09 ¬ß2.1(3) already specifies `UpgradeAuthorized { code_hash, authorized_at }` and derives `applicable_at = authorized_at + DescriptorLeadTime`, and the pallet's public event matches 02 byte-for-byte. Adding the third field would have *recreated* a contradiction both sides had already removed, so `UpgradeAuthorized` is unchanged. |
| SQ-114 | Reserve-probe economics: each probe spends real sovereign USDC on Asset Hub execution fees + response delivery (07 ¬ß8 prices this under the ops budget line: "probe fees are paid from sovereign DOT/USDC"), but no 13 row sizes the probe's fee envelope and nothing reconciles the cumulative probe spend against `ops.*` metering (08). At ~1 probe/epoch-day the drift is centimes, but the sovereign-account balance and the local issuance diverge by exactly the accumulated fees ‚Äî relevant to the NAV/backing reconciliation (08 ¬ß4) and to SQ-111's base question. Where is the probe fee envelope owned? | 07 ¬ß8; 08 ¬ß1.1; 13 ¬ß1 | 2026-07-16 (B4 review) | open ‚Äî batch **X** (**reclassified 2026-07-20**, B4 ratification ‚Äî refused; implementation owed). The row's own premise is false, so ratifying it would enshrine a wrong reason. Probe fees are **not** bounded by `res.probe_amount`: the execution leg is bounded, but the response-delivery leg uses `SetFeesMode { jit_withdraw: true }` and draws **DOT from the sovereign account outside the probe amount** (`runtime/bleavit-xcm/src/probe.rs:55-63`, asserted at `tests.rs:504-540`). `res.probe_amount` is itself uncapped (min 0, max `u128::MAX`, `max_delta None`) on a permissionless crank, and 07 ¬ß8's ‚Äúpaid from sovereign DOT/USDC under the ops budget line‚Äù is a **dangling citation** ‚Äî doc 08 contains zero occurrences of ‚Äúprobe‚Äù and `BudgetLine` has no probe line. Harmless today only because production is unwired (`RuntimeProbeDispatch = ()`). Needs a bounded DOT fee envelope in 13 (cf. `ops.coretime_fee_dot`) plus a real 08 line; sentences land in 07 ¬ß8 and 13 ¬ß1. |
| SQ-98 | Ledger-freeze force-reject: mechanism, reason code, and scope. `epoch-core::tick` force-rejects **every** live non-terminal pre-Executed proposal to `Rejected(ProcessHold)` + vault-void-at-par under `self.ledger_frozen` (`decide_with` likewise returns `ProcessHold`). This is G-1-safe (06 ¬ß6.3 "resolve to status quo"; D-1 par-void; the literal T10‚ÜíT21‚Üímeasure-REJECT path is unworkable while `market.set_frozen(true)` halts trading), but three texts point elsewhere: (1) 06 ¬ß6.3 frames the effect as "decision windows overlapping a freeze **fail decision-grade (staleness)**" ‚Äî a decide-time `NotDecisionGrade` (T10) scoped to windows that overlap, not a T20 force-reject over all live proposals; (2) 09 ¬ß1.2(10) blocks `execute()` during an active PB-LEDGER-FREEZE, implying `Queued` items **wait out** the bounded (‚â§14+14 d) freeze rather than being destroyed; (3) 05 ¬ß1.3's `ProcessHold` producer map lists T20 only as "force-reject under **VOID/stale-epoch**" and a ledger freeze is not obviously a "VOID condition". **Pre-existing** ‚Äî this behavior predates PR #56; the dead-man fix only *removed* dead-man from the tick force-reject set and deliberately left `ledger_frozen` (the dead-man/ledger asymmetry). Either narrow the trigger to decision-window proposals and let `Queued` items wait, or amend 06 ¬ß6.3 / 05 ¬ß2.1 to bless a freeze-wide T20 `ProcessHold`+void. | 06 ¬ß6.3; 05 ¬ß2.1 T20; 05 ¬ß1.3; 09 ¬ß1.2(10) | 2026-07-16 (A8+A11 PR #56 spec-review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (code fix).** The divergence is closed: `decide_with`'s ledger-freeze arm moved out of the decision chain and now shares `tick`'s exact predicate (`self.ledger_frozen \|\| self.stale_process_hold(pid)`), force-rejecting to `Rejected(ProcessHold)` with a par vault void. Both cranks therefore land on identical ledger state; previously a race decided whether Accept-branch holders recovered ¬Ω (par void) or nothing (Reject-branch resolution). Par void is also the only self-consistent choice: the freeze halts `market.crank_observe`, so `reject_to_measurement` would have sent proposals to await a score the freeze itself prevents producing. `dead_man_armed` and `guards.process_hold` were deliberately left in the decision chain (the 05 ¬ß4.8 dead-man asymmetry is intentional). Precedence note: the freeze check now precedes the static guards, so a freeze forgoes the 10 % `!preimage_ok` slash ‚Äî accepted, and conservative in the G-1 direction (no confiscation during a machine-checked solvency emergency; cf. the SQ-191 ruling that confiscation requires a verified culpable act). Spec trued: 06 ¬ß6.3 now states the T20 `ProcessHold` + par-void disposition explicitly ‚Äî the old "fail decision-grade (staleness)" wording implied `NotDecisionGrade`, which forfeits 10 % of bond under ¬ß4(3) and would fine proposers for an operational emergency; 05 ¬ß2.1 T20 and ¬ß1.3 now name the freeze as a producer and note the tick/decide equivalence. **The sweep *scope* question the row also raised is not settled here** ‚Äî whether `Queued` items should wait out the bounded freeze per 09 ¬ß1.2(10) rather than be force-rejected is split out as **SQ-286**. **Known modelling gap (deliberate, recorded not fixed):** `models/tla/proposal/Proposal.tla` binds T20 to the `Tick` action only, so the amended 05 ¬ß2.1 T20 row (which now also names `decide()`) is ahead of the model. Loosening the binding was tried and reverted ‚Äî the model carries no freeze variable, so the edge is unreachable there, and admitting `Decide` for T20 makes a Decide/Trading‚ÜíRejected/ProcessHold step match **both** T10 (`ProcessHold` ‚àà `T10Reasons`) and T20, breaking the `Cardinality(matches) = 1` clause of `TransitionTableExhaustive`. Disambiguating requires carrying the vault outcome in the transition record (T20 voids; T10 resolves into measurement), which is a modelling change owed its own pass. The reason for the binding is now commented in the model itself. |

The runtime follows 04/05's explicit 1% rule through a primitives constant, but 13 does not own that threshold or say whether the META-governed classification should instead have a dedicated `Params` key; `trs.stream_threshold` is a different grant-stream concept and is not reused. | 04 ¬ß9; 05 ¬ß5.1; 08; 13 reading rules | 2026-07-16 (A8 review; B1b extension 2026-07-17) | **closed 2026-07-20 (B4 ratification) ‚Äî obsoleted by integration contract v5.** The question no longer has a subject: the TREASURY ask/NAV gate threshold was **deleted** on 2026-07-19 (commit `243a1f9`) when v5 made gate markets universal across every market-bearing class. `requires_gate_markets` is now a compile-time class match with no NAV read (`crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:45-50`); `TREASURY_GATE_NAV_BPS` and the `treasury_gate_required` seam are gone from the tree. 13 ¬ß1 explicitly records that universal gating introduces **no new parameter**, so there is no threshold for 13 to own. Docs and code already agree ‚Äî no amendment made. |
| SQ-86 | The stale-epoch force-reject bound (`StaleEpochBound`, 05 ¬ß2.1 T20 / 13 ¬ß2) has no spec-defined anchor. A8 latches a high-water proposal-id snapshot when the persisted next phase boundary is overdue by `StaleEpochBound` (epoch-staleness, **not** a per-proposal lifetime); affected proposals (id ‚â§ cutoff) force-reject, later submissions are immune. | 05 ¬ß2.1 T20; 05 ¬ß3.3; 13 ¬ß2 | 2026-07-16 (A8 review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß2.1 gained a normative *T20 stale-epoch anchor* note ratifying the shipped mechanism: `StaleEpochBound` measures **epoch staleness, not per-proposal lifetime** ‚Äî the overdue margin on the persisted **next phase** boundary ‚Äî and trips a latched high-water proposal-id snapshot, so `id <= cutoff` force-rejects and later submissions are immune. The note also pins two behaviours the row omitted and code-verification surfaced: the latch is **suppressed while the dead-man is armed/paused or during recovery** (a stalled clock the dead-man already owns must not also be charged to epoch staleness), and it **self-clears** once the drain completes, making it a bounded drain rather than a permanent mode. |

No ambiguity remains; a future pre-qual detail surface would be a new contract feature. |

Append to 02 ¬ß6 at a future contract touch. | 05 ¬ß5.3; 02 ¬ß6 | 2026-07-16 (A8 review) | open ‚Äî **ruled 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6); reclassified to X carrying its implementation scope.** The contract half landed: `BaselineCarried { pid, epoch }` is frozen in 02 ¬ß6 under inclusion rule (b). **The row's load-bearing claim is false** ‚Äî it says A8 sources the previous settled Baseline TWAP "via a market seam"; the production adapter actually searches `RecentCohortSummaries` for epoch e‚àí1 (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/configs.rs`), and that cohort cannot settle until e+2. So the adapter returns `None` on the normal schedule and a failed current Baseline takes `Reject(NotDecisionGrade)` instead of carrying: **05 ¬ß5.3's carry path is unreachable in production**, and the pallet test only reaches it because the mock injects `PreviousBaselineTwap` directly. Owed in X: a spec-consistent source available by epoch e's decision, plus a real-schedule test (first carry emits, second consecutive carry rejects). |
| SQ-89 | The cohort-VOID path (06 ¬ß6.2 `epoch.void_cohort`; 03 ¬ß2.3 Resolved‚ÜíVoided during measurement) has no 02 ¬ß6 event, and 05 does not state whether cohort proposals also change state. A8 emits `CohortVoided`, transitions each cohort proposal to terminal `Rejected(ProcessHold)` + vault `Voided`, and counts only **non-terminal** cohorts toward I-21. | 06 ¬ß6.2; 03 ¬ß2.3; 05 ¬ß3.3; 02 ¬ß6 | 2026-07-16 (A8 review) | resolved 2026-07-21 (batch C, contract v6) ‚Äî `CohortVoided { epoch: EpochId }` is frozen in 02 ¬ß6 under the new inclusion rule (c): a cohort VOID is a terminal cohort transition, and ¬ß6's own completeness claim already required an event for it. 05 now states the disposition normatively (every affected measuring/same-epoch live proposal is archived `Reject(ProcessHold)`, emits `ProposalForceRejected`, moves any market-backed vault to `Voided`, and leaves the non-terminal set) and T20's event name is corrected from `ProposalRejected` to `ProposalForceRejected`. Behaviour was already shipped and correct, including the I-21 non-terminal counting. |
| SQ-90 | 05 ¬ß4.8's dead-man recovery epoch is under-specified ‚Äî it does not define recovery-epoch indexing / deadline re-anchoring or whether recovery also blocks withdrawal / execution callbacks / expiry / guardian transitions. A8 blocks qualify/open_markets/decide/rerun-open during recovery, carries pre-pause `Submitted` proposals into the first post-recovery epoch, and runs exactly one proposal-free epoch. | 05 ¬ß4.8 | 2026-07-16 (A8 review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling; corrected 2026-07-20 after Codex P2 review of PR #114).** 05 ¬ß4.8 gained a normative *recovery epoch* paragraph. The refused set is stated exactly ‚Äî `submit`, `qualify`, `open_markets`, `decide`, rerun-open and `settle_cohort` fail with a phase error while recovery is set (the row's list omitted `submit` and `settle_cohort`). **The first ruling over-ratified the open set and was corrected against `epoch-core` before merge:** execution callbacks (`mark_executed`, `mark_failed_executed`, retry exhaustion, `veto_upheld`) and guardian transitions (`delay_once`, `force_rerun`, `schedule_rerun`, vault voiding) carry no recovery guard and are genuinely open, but **withdrawal is categorically closed for the entire recovery epoch** ‚Äî `withdraw` requires `proposal.epoch == CurrentEpoch` (`crates/epoch-core/src/lib.rs:737`), resume increments the clock so recovery is a fresh index R = P+1 (`lib.rs:608-614`), and `submit` is refused throughout, making that guard unsatisfiable for every proposal; and **expiry is open only in its on-demand form** ‚Äî the dedicated `expire_or_stale_queue` entry point stays callable, while the automatic `tick` sweep that normally drives it no-ops for recovery's duration (`lib.rs:1430`). Carry-forward is defined by the observed clock (pre-pause `Submitted` proposals re-anchor to whatever epoch the crank observes, so a late crank lands them past recovery+1), and "exactly one proposal-free epoch" is explicitly conditioned on no re-trigger. The G-1 question the correction exposes ‚Äî whether recovery *should* offer an intake-bond exit rather than deferring it one epoch ‚Äî is split out as **SQ-285** rather than settled by ratification. |
| SQ-91 | 05 ¬ß2.1 T5/T6 do not define equal-bond tie-breaking or the exact rollover-exhaustion representation. A8 ranks qualification bond-descending then `pid`-ascending; a no-slot / lock-conflict rolls once then refunds. | 05 ¬ß2.1 | 2026-07-16 (A8 review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (B1 ratification, delegated ruling).** 05 ¬ß2.1 gained a normative *T5/T6 ordering and rollover exhaustion* note: rank is **bond-descending, then `pid`-ascending** (the tie-break is deterministic submission order, so equal bonds never depend on iteration order), and a proposal rolls over **exactly once** before cancelling with a full refund. The ruling additionally pins what the row did not ask but the code does: the single rollover allowance is **per proposal, not per cause** ‚Äî a proposal deferred once by slot contention and then demoted by the 08 ¬ß4.4 POL-budget shrink has already spent it. |

A11 defines + models the constraint that only atomic `batch_all` is admitted; **B1a's concrete SafetyFilter MUST reject `utility.batch`/`force_batch`** in guard payloads. | 09 ¬ß1.2(12); 09 ¬ß1.4; 15 I-11 | 2026-07-16 (A11 review) | **resolved 2026-07-20 (triage sweep ‚Äî code-verified).** The guard seam rejects both best-effort wrappers **recursively**, including when nested inside `batch_all` (`runtime/bleavit-runtime/src/classifier.rs:786-791` in `collect_guard_domains`, reached from `safety_filter` and re-run at dispatch).
